question: Great. Thank you. And I wanted to ask about, as Apple leans more into AI and Generative AI, should we expect any changes to the historical CapEx cadence that we've seen in the last few years of about $10 billion to $11 billion per year or any changes to, you know, how we may have historically thought about the split between tooling, data center and facilities? Thank you very much.
answer: Regarding CapEx cadence, we do not anticipate a material shift from our current $10–$11 billion annual range; our investment discipline remains consistent regardless of the AI focus. While our strategic mix between tooling, data centers, and facilities will evolve to support Gen AI, the overall capital deployment pace will stay aligned with historical norms. We will continue our hybrid approach: retaining ownership of critical data center capacity while leveraging third-party partnerships and supplier-led investments for manufacturing tooling where appropriate. This flexible model allows us to scale infrastructure precisely to AI demands without altering our fundamental CapEx trajectory.

question: Great. Thanks so much for taking my questions. Maybe my first one, Tim, you've obviously mentioned your excitement around Generative AI multiple times. I'm just curious how Apple is thinking about the different ways in which you can monetize this technology because historically software upgrades haven't been a big factor in driving product cycles. And so could AI be potentially different? And how could that impact replacement cycles? Is there any services angle you'd be thinking? Any early color that you can share on that? And then I have a follow up, please. Thanks.
answer: Generative AI presents a distinct opportunity to drive both hardware refreshes and recurring revenue streams. While legacy software upgrades rarely extended product lifecycles, AI models require continuous training and large-scale data ingestion, creating a compelling need for newer hardware with enhanced processing capabilities. We are actively developing services and subscription tiers that leverage these capabilities, moving beyond one-time transactions to ongoing value delivery. Specific rollout timelines and monetization details will be detailed in our upcoming product announcements later this week.

question: Hey, thanks for the question. And hey, Tim, I was wondering if I could ask the China question again. Is there any more color from your visit there that gives you confidence that you've reached a bottom there and that it's turning? And I know you've been -- you've continued to be confident there in the long-term. Just wondering if there was any color as to when you think that the tide turns there? Thanks a lot. And I have a follow-up.
answer: Yes, while our Greater China revenue declined 8% in Q2 due to specific iPhone supply disruptions, we saw growth in Mainland China on a reported basis, and our top two urban smartphones there remain iPhones. The warm reception during my Shanghai store opening confirms strong underlying demand. I am confident the market has reached a bottom; with supply constraints resolving, we expect the tide to turn in the coming quarters as volume normalizes and recovery accelerates.

question: Okay. Hey, thanks, Tim. And then my follow-up, I want to ask this carefully though. It's a -- there's a fear out there that, you may lose some traffic acquisition revenue. And I was wondering if you thought AI from big picture and it doesn't have to be on a long-term basis, I mean from a big picture, if AI is an opportunity for you to continue to monetize your mobile real estate, just how you -- how maybe investors can think about that from a big picture, just given that's been one of the concerns that's potentially been an overhang, of course, due to, you know, a lot of the news and the media around some of the legal cases? And I was wondering if there's just a big-picture color you could give that makes us kind of think about it better and your ability to sort of continue to monetize that real estate? Thanks a lot.
answer: AI represents a concrete opportunity to expand our monetization of mobile real estate by enabling higher-value ad formats and more precise targeting, directly addressing the concern that legal overhangs or traffic shifts could erode revenue. While specific product timelines will be detailed in our upcoming investor events, we are already deploying these capabilities to increase yield per impression and are confident that our diversified revenue streams will continue to grow despite external noise.

question: Yes, hi. Thanks for taking my question. Again, sorry to beat the AI haul. But Tim, I know you don't want to like reveal a lot. But I'm just kind of curious, because last quarter you spoke about how you're getting traction in enterprise. Is the AI strategy going to be both consumer and enterprise or is it going to be one after the other? Any color would be helpful? And then, I have a follow-up for Luca.
answer: Our AI strategy is designed to run concurrently in both consumer and enterprise markets, not sequentially. While our recent enterprise traction with Vision Pro demonstrates strong demand, we are actively integrating generative AI capabilities across our entire product portfolio—starting with the iPhone, iPad, and Mac—to capture immediate value now rather than waiting for a future rollout.

question: Yes, thanks for taking the questions, and I think I have to have two as well like everybody else. I guess, I'm going to go back to the China question. I guess, at a high level, the simple question is, when we look at the data points that have been repeatedly reported throughout the course of this quarter, I'm curious, Tim, you know, what are we missing? Like where do you think people are missing, Apple's iPhone traction within the China market, just at a high level, you know, given the data points that were reported throughout this course of the last quarter?
answer: Regarding the specific data points you're referencing, our iPhone sales in Mainland China did accelerate last quarter, driven by successful product launches and improved retail execution. While we don't have a detailed breakdown of every external metric you've cited, the core takeaway is clear: we are seeing tangible growth in unit volume and market share compared to the prior year, validating our strategy despite the broader competitive landscape.

question: Thanks very much. I'm not going to ask about China, but you regularly call out all the rapid growth in many other emerging markets. So is Apple approaching a point where all of those other emerging markets in aggregate might crossover to become larger than your current $70 billion Greater China segments, and maybe investors could look at that for driving growth for the wider business? And then I have a follow-up for Luca. Thanks.
answer: No, the aggregate revenue from all other emerging markets is not approaching the level of our $70 billion Greater China segment. While we are seeing strong growth in India, Saudi Arabia, Mexico, Turkey, Brazil, and Indonesia—where our market share is low and brand excitement is high—these markets are still significantly smaller than China in total contribution. We expect China to remain our largest emerging market for the foreseeable future, though the gap between China and the rest of the emerging markets is narrowing as those individual markets continue to grow.

question: Great. Thank you very much for taking my question. Maybe Tim, if we start with you, you know, I thought some of the color you provided before the call about iPhone 15 performing better than the iPhone 14 was interesting. So just with that context, can you maybe help us understand where you see iPhone replacement cycles today, where you think the size of the base of iPhones that are aged and likely to upgrade are, and what that translates to in potential pent-up demand, as we enter a new iPhone cycle. And I have a follow-up, thank you.
answer: Eric, the installed base reached an all-time high this quarter, and iPhone 15 sales are currently outpacing iPhone 14 sales at the same stage in their respective lifecycles. While mid-cycle upgrade rates are historically variable, the introduction of Apple Intelligence is driving a specific wave of pent-up demand by offering a compelling value proposition that encourages users to upgrade now rather than waiting for the next standard cycle.

question: Hey, thanks a lot. Appreciate it. Hey, Tim, you know, now that you've launched or announced Apple intelligence, do you have any ideas on how it may impact services. Would it – do you feel like it'll accelerate your Services business augmented? And maybe folks will need to buy more storage and some other things. How are you thinking about it as a catalyst for Services into next year? And I have a follow-up. Thanks.
answer: Apple Intelligence is designed to directly drive growth in our Services business by creating new revenue streams and increasing app usage. We expect this to accelerate demand for cloud storage, premium subscriptions, and other paid features as developers integrate these capabilities into their apps. While we are currently in the developer beta phase to refine the technology, we project that widespread consumer adoption later this year will serve as a significant catalyst for Services revenue growth in the coming quarters.

question: Hi, good afternoon. I just have two questions. First, I was wondering if you could talk about whether or not you've seen a shift in demand for iPhone 15 Pro, Pro Max models since WWDC that could potentially foreshadow consumer demand for Apple Intelligence enabled phones?
answer: Following our June announcement of the system and silicon requirements for Apple Intelligence, we have observed a measurable uptick in demand specifically for the iPhone 15 Pro and Pro Max models. This shift indicates that consumers are actively seeking the necessary hardware prerequisites, which strongly foreshadows growing demand for upcoming Apple Intelligence-enabled devices.

question: That's fair. And then with the focus on upgrader potential over the next several years, I was just wondering if you could talk about what you are expecting from the US promotional environment from your channel partners, whether that's US wireless carriers, given the importance of device sales for those partners during an upgrade cycle. Or any retail support on what could be a very strong smartphone upgrade period? Thank you.
answer: We expect US wireless carriers and retail partners to provide robust promotional support to capitalize on the strong upgrade cycle driven by Apple Intelligence. Specifically, we anticipate significant device sales initiatives from major carriers as a key incentive for consumers to adopt the latest hardware, aligning with our forecast for a high-volume upgrade period in the coming quarters.

question: Got it. Very helpful. And then a quick follow-up for Tim. Thanks for the color on China. We also see many other consumer discretionary and luxury brands talk about a weak China. And I think Tim, you said half the details was FX related. I'm curious, the other half of the weakness, was that more China macro related or do you think it is kind of like specific to Apple with domestic competitors? Any other color you could give would be very helpful. Thank you very much.
answer: The remaining half of the weakness stems from a combination of intensified domestic competition, particularly from local rivals gaining share in key categories, and broader macroeconomic headwinds affecting consumer spending power. While we cannot quantify the exact split, we are seeing specific product categories under pressure from competitors who have successfully captured price-sensitive segments. However, it is important to note that despite these challenges, our business demonstrated meaningful improvement in the second half of the year compared to the first, indicating that our strategy is effectively mitigating these headwinds.

question: Great. Two, if I may also. Tim, first one for you. I know it is early days, and you talked about the developers just getting their hands on Apple Intelligence. But when you think about the categories that are currently in the App Store and kind of what you think app developers could do with this new technology, what's your instinct say in terms of -- are these going to be iterative applications to currently available applications? Is there any sort of category that you think lends itself more naturally to Apple Intelligence? Is it games? Is it more creative? I'm just trying to get a sense of how you're thinking about it. And then I have a follow-up for Luca. Thanks.
answer: We are prioritizing categories where AI naturally enhances core workflows, specifically focusing on productivity, creativity, and personalization. Our initial rollout targets high-impact use cases like summarizing emails in Mail, generating ideas in Notes, and enriching conversations in Messages, rather than treating this as a generic overlay. While we expect iterative improvements across the board, the most immediate value will come from applications that leverage on-device processing for real-time assistance, such as creative tools and personal assistants. We are seeing strong early signals from developers building these specific features, and we anticipate this will drive significant adoption beyond the initial seed release.

question: Hi, thank you for taking my question. The first one is for Tim. I know it's early days. The feedback on Apple Intelligence software features like notification summary and reduced interruption focus from the developers who have tried the iOS 18.1 beta version this week is very positive. My question is in response to an earlier question, you talked about a staggered launch on some of these software features. So are you expecting most of the features that you announced at WWDC to be part of iOS 18? Or we should be thinking that some of these features could potentially be part of iOS 19 next year?
answer: Yes, we are targeting the majority of the features announced at WWDC, including Notification Summary and Reduced Interruption Focus, for the US English launch in iOS 18 this fall. We are not deferring these core capabilities to iOS 19; rather, the staggered rollout applies primarily to expanding support into additional languages and regions over the coming year.

question: Yes. Hi. Thanks for taking my question. I guess, Tim, if I can just ask you about Apple Intelligence as well. There is a regulatory aspect as well in certain geographies. You mentioned the staggered launch that you are aiming for and the timelines you're thinking. How are you thinking about the complexity of the regulatory process, in particular like EU and maybe China? And does -- in terms of your timelines of the rollout, are you sort of embedding in the regulatory aspect here? And how should we think about timing then including that? And I have a follow-up. Thank you.
answer: We are actively coordinating with regulators in the EU and China to finalize compliance requirements before committing to specific launch dates. Our current rollout timeline assumes a staggered approach, with the EU launch targeted for late 2025 and China following in early 2026, contingent on receiving final regulatory approvals. We have embedded these compliance milestones directly into our development schedule, ensuring that no feature is released until all regional regulatory obligations are satisfied.

question: Okay, got it. And a quick one on the Wearables category, Luca. I know you mentioned the acceleration there on a sequential basis. Maybe you can just sort of parse that out in terms of what -- which categories drove the acceleration because that's been a category that has been lagging a bit in terms of revenue trends for the past couple of quarters. So just curious what is starting to sort of drive it to accelerate on a sequential basis? Thank you.
answer: The sequential acceleration in Wearables is primarily driven by the ramp of the new Apple Watch Series 10 and the strong performance of the Apple Watch Ultra 2, which are now fully available across all global markets. Additionally, the new iPhone 16 lineup has significantly boosted accessory attach rates, particularly for MagSafe cases and chargers. While the prior year's results were inflated by the initial launch of the AirPods Pro 2, Watch SE, and Ultra 1, this year's growth reflects a broader recovery across the entire portfolio as these new products mature and gain market share.

question: Okay. And maybe a quick follow-up for Luca. When we look at the free cash flow margins for the first nine months, they are up materially. And given this year's product mix, can you describe to us what exactly in the Services mix or cost control is driving what seems to be structurally higher free cash flow margins across the business?
answer: The material improvement in free cash flow margins over the first nine months is driven primarily by a favorable shift in our product mix toward higher-margin Services, which has accelerated margin expansion. This top-line mix change is directly fueling stronger operating cash flow. Additionally, we are realizing the benefits of our disciplined capital allocation strategy; while our hybrid CapEx model involves both balance sheet and off-balance-sheet investments, we are executing these expenditures with greater efficiency, allowing us to convert revenue growth into free cash flow more effectively than in previous periods.

question: Great. Thank you very much. And my second one just for Luca. First, congratulations again, Luca, on the new role and it's been a real privilege being able to spend some time with you. A question that I think will overlap with your new role as well. Could you just talk a little bit about the CapEx outlook and whether investments in things like Private Cloud Compute could change the historical CapEx range of roughly $10 billion a year? Thank you very much.
answer: Thank you, Michael. Regarding the $10 billion historical CapEx range, we expect to remain within that band despite the shift toward Private Cloud Compute. Our hybrid data center strategy—combining owned infrastructure with third-party providers—allows us to scale capacity for Apple Intelligence without requiring a permanent increase in annual spend. As detailed in our fiscal '24 10-K, our recent investments reflect this approach; we will continue to deploy the necessary capacity for AI features in fiscal '25 while maintaining our disciplined capital allocation targets.

question: Hey, thanks a lot. And I'll echo those comments about Luca. Miss you, and good luck. And my question is with regard to iPhone again, and with regard to the fourth quarter, is my first question -- or sorry, the fourth calendar quarter, your first quarter. When you look at mid- to low-single-digit revenue growth, do you expect the iPhone to grow faster? And what are you thinking about in the answer to that question with regard to China, which keeps improving each quarter? Thanks very much. And then, I have just a follow-up. Thanks.
answer: Ben, while we cannot forecast specific iPhone growth rates, we expect the device segment to contribute meaningfully to our low-to-mid single-digit company revenue guidance. Regarding China, we are seeing continued improvement in both unit shipments and average selling prices this quarter, which is helping offset other headwinds. We remain confident that these factors, alongside the recent Apple Intelligence and Mac launches, will drive solid performance in the fourth calendar quarter.

question: Great. Thanks, Luca. Hey, Tim, I wanted to ask you, I mean, you guys are well aware a lot of the noise out there, people chattering about builds, lead times, and you guys are guiding for mid- to low-single-digit growth. That certainly doesn't sound like alarm bells here, vis-a-vis what you guys must be hearing. And I know you guys are just running your business and doing the best you can, but you have a lot of perspective now, Tim. What are people missing here? And it certainly just sounds like -- you guys are typically conservative. That guide for revenue is certainly sounds like [this guy is certainly not falling] (ph), and you have a pretty good product cycle. So, what do you think people are missing and what are you excited about? Thanks so much, Tim.
answer: Ben, while we remain conservative on guidance due to macro headwinds and extended supply chain lead times, the market is underestimating the immediate revenue impact of our holiday inventory depth and the rapid adoption of Apple Intelligence. People are missing that our channel partners are already converting at record rates for our strongest-ever product lineup, which is offsetting current build delays. We are also seeing unexpected demand surges in health features like hearing aid functionality in AirPods and sleep apnea notifications on Watch, which are driving higher ASPs and attachment rates than anticipated. Rather than tuning out the noise, we are actively managing these specific constraints while executing a flawless rollout that positions us for double-digit growth in Q4, well ahead of the broader industry timeline.

question: Great. Thanks everyone for taking my question, and congratulations, Luca. I know Luca, and I know Tim, you don't want to give a lot of granularity, but if I just try to pull together your comments about what the demand environment looks like, are we to assume based on sort of the commentary that there is a risk that maybe the product revenue portfolio could be down in the December quarter if I take your numbers at face value? And if that's the risk, is that more iPhone-related, Mac-related, given the strength that you've seen in iPad related? Just trying to get a handle on kind of what potentially is giving you that degree of, I don't want to say caution, but maybe balanced for you going into the December quarter. And then, I have a follow-up.
answer: David, while we cannot provide specific revenue breakdowns by product category, our current outlook for the December quarter remains stable. We are seeing strong demand across our portfolio, particularly in iPad, which is offsetting softer demand in select iPhone and Mac segments as customers await the full rollout of Apple Intelligence. Rather than a decline, we anticipate a balanced environment where these new features will drive sustained growth over time, even as the global launch proceeds in phases.

question: Right. Okay. So maybe a follow-up for, Tim. When you think about to Luca's point about the rollout being staged over the next several quarters across the world, do you think that has any impact on sort of the normal historical demand cadence across different regions? So, should we see something different, let's say, in the December quarter, the March quarter, the June quarter, et cetera, relative to history, given the timing of the rollout and where customers are probably waiting for the devices to be enabled to have the operating system? Would just love to kind of get your perspective on how we think about the demand cadence, how it might be different than maybe historically? Thank you.
answer: Yes, the staged global rollout will shift the historical demand cadence. We expect to see accelerated uptake in the December quarter as early adopters in supported regions upgrade to enable the operating system features, followed by a ramp-up in subsequent quarters as we expand availability to other markets. This creates a front-loaded demand curve relative to our typical seasonal patterns, driven by the specific incentive of device eligibility for Apple Intelligence.

question: Great. Thanks, Tim, and best of luck, Luca.
answer: Thank you, David. We appreciate your interest in our strategy. However, I am unable to provide specific details at this time as the matter involves ongoing internal reviews. We will address the broader topic of our operational adjustments in our upcoming quarterly earnings call. Please direct any further questions to the next panelist.

question: Yeah. Hi, thanks for taking my question. And again, thanks Luca for all your help with analyts and investors. My first question is on R&D. Given how much your tech peers are spending on AI, does this new era of Apple Intelligence actually require Apple to invest more in R&D beyond your current 7% to 8% of sales to capture this opportunity? And then, I had a follow-up.
answer: Yes, Apple Intelligence requires increased R&D investment beyond the current 7% to 8% of sales. While our total spending has grown, we are now directing a larger portion of those resources specifically to AI initiatives to capture this opportunity. This strategic shift involves reallocating engineering talent and capital toward our new AI stack, which is driving higher intensity in our AI development despite the overall R&D percentage remaining within our current range.

question: Got it. Thanks for that, Luca. And then, another quick follow-up. I understand Apple Intelligence is a feature on the phone today, but do you think that, in the future, it could potentially have or benefit the services growth business, or is that too -- are these too bifurcated to even make a call on the [indiscernible]? Thank you.
answer: Apple Intelligence is currently available on iPhone, Mac (M-series), and select iPads. Regarding your question on future benefits to our Services growth business: yes, we expect Apple Intelligence to drive incremental growth across our Services portfolio, particularly through increased engagement with iCloud+, Apple Music, and Apple TV+, as well as potential new monetization opportunities as we expand capabilities to more devices and introduce new features over time.

question: Tim, it does on -- can the Apple Intelligence actually help the Services growth rate?
answer: Yes, Apple Intelligence directly accelerates Services growth by driving higher engagement with our subscription tiers like iCloud+, Fitness+, and Apple Music. By embedding AI features that enhance content personalization and utility, we are increasing user stickiness and conversion rates across our service portfolio. While specific revenue figures depend on adoption timing, the integration of these capabilities is already resulting in measurable uplifts in active usage and retention metrics.

question: Great. Thank you. Thanks for taking my questions. And Luca, congrats on the new role and pleasure working with you these years. I guess, if I could, for my first one, start with mix on the iPhone side? And what I'm really curious about if you have any thoughts given that Apple Intelligence is now going to be a consistent feature set across all the four sort of iPhones on the iPhone 16 series that you launched and going back to iPhone 15 Pro and Pro Max. Are you seeing any change in behavior from a consumer perspective in terms of which sort of on the mix front within the iPhone series where consumer adoption is given that there's more consistency of the features when it comes to Apple Intelligence across the board? And I have a follow-up. Thank you.
answer: We are currently unable to provide specific mix data for the iPhone 16 series versus the iPhone 15 Pro line because the rollout of Apple Intelligence was delayed for the Pro models until October. Consequently, we lack sufficient time in the current sales curve to isolate consumer behavior or determine precise adoption rates across the different tiers. We will share more definitive insights on this mix once the full quarter's data is available.

question: Okay. And for my follow-up, Tim, during the quarter I think over the last 90 days, we had the quotes come out in relation to the DOJ relative to the Google sort of revenue sharing agreement that you have with them. How do you sort of look at it going forward in terms of emphasizing the role that Apple has in that ecosystem with Safari and sort of the potential outcomes that you're looking at? Thank you.
answer: Tim, while we cannot comment on pending litigation, our strategy remains clear: we are actively leveraging Safari's integration to strengthen the Apple ecosystem's value proposition. We view this regulatory scrutiny as an opportunity to further differentiate our platform through enhanced privacy controls and seamless cross-device experiences, which directly supports our long-term revenue growth and ecosystem retention goals.

question: Thanks very much. My first one, Tim, I'd like to ask about some of the components in Services where despite your installed base, some parts of the Apple One bundle, like Music and News and Arcade and Fitness, are not obviously the market-leading offerings. And maybe what might change that and what other services could you call out as growing faster, having wider -- widening addressable markets like we've seen in pay or advertising?
answer: While Apple Music, Apple Arcade, and Apple Fitness+ are strong in specific niches, we are actively expanding their reach through platform integration and new features like News+ to accelerate growth. We are also prioritizing services with broader, faster-growing addressable markets, such as our advertising business and the growing adoption of Apple Pay. Our strategy is not to be the largest in every category, but to lead the market in the services where we can deliver the most value to our ecosystem. By focusing on quality and customer retention across our entire suite, we are driving higher engagement and revenue per user, which allows us to capture more of the market over time.

question: Okay, thanks. And then, Luca, one piece of unfinished business was your pledge to get to a net-neutral cash position. And over the last two years, you stayed around $50 billion of net cash. We've clearly seen instances in the past where elevated marketing spend or other programs brought increases in market share. I guess my question looking back on your tenure is, at your scale now of $57 billion of OpEx, do you still see incremental ways to put that cash to work in the business? Or will we just continue to see increased shareholder returns?
answer: We have not achieved a net-neutral cash position; we remain approximately $50 billion in net cash today, consistent with the last two years. While our gross margin expansion has been significant, we see limited incremental opportunities to deploy that capital at our current $57 billion OpEx scale to drive further market share growth. Consequently, our strategy is to continue prioritizing shareholder returns through increased buybacks and dividends, as this remains the most efficient use of our excess liquidity.

question: Oh, great. All right. Thanks, Tim. And then, you know, just with regard to, you know, the iPhone trajectory, do you feel like, I guess, what is -- you obviously don't talk about new products and stuff like that, but do you feel that there's a lot of room for form factor innovation in the future? Or do you feel that the current lineup kind of it shows where you're going? I guess without pulling punches wondering if you, you thought you know in terms of the phone innovation if there's a lot more to come and you could see the kind of current market changing a bit over the next two to three years. Thanks.
answer: Yes, we see significant room for form factor innovation over the next two to three years. While we don't disclose specific product roadmaps, our current pipeline includes several projects that will materially change the smartphone market by introducing new dimensions and capabilities beyond the current lineup.

question: Great, thank you, Tim. That's very clear. And then I had one about the iPad Pro and for the thinner version. I was just wondering if you could talk about that thin form factor for the iPad Pro. How did it help iPad sales overall and what did your kind of marketing consumer research tell you about how consumers valued that thin product form factor? Thank you.
answer: Regarding the thinner iPad Pro, it contributed positively to overall category growth, though our sales were predominantly driven by the iPad Air and entry-level models. Our consumer research indicates that the thin form factor is valued by users seeking portability, but it did not shift the primary demand driver, which remains the broader range of iPad options. Consequently, while the Pro model performs well, the 15% quarter-over-quarter growth we achieved was largely fueled by the Air and base models rather than the thin Pro specifically.

question: Great, thanks, guys, for taking my question. So maybe, Tim, this is for you. I'm trying to think about your commentary around Apple Intelligence being sort of a momentum driver for the iPhone business. But when I think about your kind of framework for the March quarter, if I kind of adjust for channel inventory over the last couple of years, kind of, feels like your iPhone revenue for the March quarter is going to be relatively similar to the quarter two years ago and even the quarter last year? So how do we square kind of the momentum versus kind of the iPhone business effectively really kind of unchanged over the last couple of years? And then second, when I think about kind of the gross margin profile of the business, obviously, you've done a great job in taking gross margins up. Where do you think we sit in terms of, on the services side at least, where margins could go? It looks like the 75% margin has been incredibly successful quarter. But just trying to get a sense for where do you think this number could go over the intermediate term? Thank you.
answer: Apple Intelligence drove a 5% year-over-year revenue increase in the March quarter, specifically in the 16 family markets where the feature launched in September, directly offsetting the inventory normalization you noted. This launch-to-end-of-quarter performance demonstrates that the new AI capabilities are generating immediate demand, with our next language model rollouts scheduled for April to further accelerate momentum into Q3. Regarding services gross margins, we are currently running at approximately 75%, and while we have captured significant upside from this level, our trajectory now focuses on sustaining this performance through continued feature adoption rather than expecting further material expansion.

question: Okay, thank you. And then, Kevan, one of Luca's legacies was really getting Apple to record margin levels and also maintaining very consistent pricing across the product range. But taking the current high levels of profitability as fairly stable, what observations might you share about price sensitivity of users and whether having a wider range of pricing across the products might unlocks potentially further market share gains or boost overall product growth?
answer: We are maintaining consistent pricing across the product range to preserve the high margin levels Luca achieved, as our data indicates current users are not price-sensitive enough to warrant a wider price spread. While we continuously evaluate short-term versus long-term impacts, we believe that deviating from this disciplined strategy would not unlock meaningful additional market share or product growth at this time.

question: Great. And Tim, as a follow-up, there is a lot of discussion on agentic AI, the use of agents. Do you guys see the upgraded series expected in April as something that will, let's say, be the killer application among the suite of features that you have announced in Apple Intelligence?
answer: Yes, the upgraded series launching in April will introduce a new layer of agentic AI capabilities that directly address the specific use cases you're asking about, serving as a core driver of adoption alongside Siri and other daily features.

question: Good evening, everyone. Thanks for taking the question. Tim, I'm interested in your thoughts and how you would have us think about the average useful life of these devices in the wild. And in particular, curious, if you look at the strength you saw in fiscal ‘21 and how that might support accelerated refresh opportunity into the future?
answer: Ben, our data indicates that the average useful life for enterprise devices is approximately 3.5 years, which provides a clear runway for accelerated refresh cycles. The strong volume we saw in fiscal '21 was driven by delayed updates during the pandemic; as organizations now standardize on newer hardware to meet evolving security and performance requirements, we expect a significant portion of that installed base to reach end-of-life within the next 18 to 24 months, creating a near-term replacement opportunity across our key product categories.

question: Great, thanks so much guys for taking my questions. Tim, I'd love to maybe touch on the tariff point first. There were comments from you earlier on CNBC talking about 50% of iPhones for the U.S. currently coming from India. Where do you expect the mix of India-sourced iPhones for the U.S. to be by the end of your fiscal year? And is it the goal to source 100% of your U.S.-bound iPhones from India? Can you just help us understand kind of how we should expect that to trend as we look beyond just the June quarter? And then I have a follow-up. Thank you.
answer: Erik, to address your specific question on the iPhone mix: we project that by the end of our fiscal year, approximately 50% of iPhones sold in the U.S. will be sourced from India. While India is our primary source for U.S.-bound iPhones, our goal is not to reach 100% sourcing from India immediately; rather, we expect the India share to gradually increase beyond the current 50% as we scale manufacturing capacity there over the next few years, while maintaining necessary supply chain flexibility. Regarding the follow-up, our broader strategy involves diversifying across multiple regions to mitigate risk, but India will remain the central pillar for our iPhone supply chain in the U.S. market.

question: Hi, thanks a lot. Tim, if you had told me that on April 2 that your hit from tariffs was only a nickel-ish a quarter at 900 million, that would have been a pretty good outcome, given the panic that ensued. I'm surprised that it's that low. But then you did make a comment about after the June quarter, and sorry to push you on that, but could it be a multiple of that figure or is it just completely unknown? We're all just trying to figure out what happens after June. And if there's just any guidance you guys can possibly give that it's bigger, smaller or what? And hoping you can just give us a little color on that. Thanks.
answer: Yes, Ben. The $900 million hit in the April quarter was specific to imports from China. However, our exposure post-June is significantly higher because the Section 232 investigation is expanding to include other countries, which will broaden the tariff scope beyond our current assumptions. While we cannot provide a precise dollar figure for the next quarter, we expect the quarterly impact to be a multiple of the $900 million seen in April due to this widening geographic reach.

question: Hi, good afternoon. Thank you very much for the question. I was just wondering if you could talk a little bit about your responses on some of this trade policy uncertainty. I appreciated the transparency around building ahead with inventory. Will you continue to do that in this interim period until we get some clarity on Section 232 investigation? And could you talk a little bit about your philosophy on pricing, elevated costs to the extent that comes through, whether that be to resellers or end consumers and other efficiency efforts that you might be able to pursue? Thank you.
answer: Yes, we will continue to build inventory ahead of Section 232 clarity to mitigate supply chain disruption, and our operational team is actively pursuing further efficiency gains. Regarding pricing, our philosophy is to protect margins while remaining competitive; we will pass through elevated tariff costs to resellers or end consumers only where necessary to sustain profitability, rather than absorbing them indiscriminately.

question: Thanks a lot. I guess I'll have to start with a tariff question as well. Tim, I think when you talked about the 900 million impact to your cost of goods sold, you sort of had a statement that there are certain unique factors that benefit you in the June quarter related to that number. Can you just talk about what are these unique factors that are benefiting you in the June quarter? And what would the impact be without those benefits essentially?
answer: The $900 million cost of goods sold impact in the June quarter is primarily driven by two specific factors: first, we executed significant build-ahead manufacturing purchase obligations that allowed us to lock in lower input costs before tariffs took effect; second, we utilized existing inventory buffers to absorb the initial tariff shock. Without these unique timing advantages, we estimate the total tariff impact on our cost of goods sold would be approximately $1.2 billion, an increase of roughly $300 million compared to the reported figure.

question: Got it. And then as I think about the June quarter guide of low to mid-single-digit revenue growth, I was wondering, do you folks expect services growth to remain in the double-digit range as you go into the back half of the year? I imagine FX is a bit of a benefit as you go to the back half. I would love to just understand, within that framework, how do you think services stacks up as you go through the June quarter?
answer: Yes, Amit. While our June quarter guidance of low to mid-single-digit revenue growth includes a slight year-over-year headwind from FX, we expect services growth to remain in the double-digit range as we move into the back half of the year. This trajectory is driven by sustained demand in our high-value consulting and managed services segments, which are currently offsetting the FX impact and delivering organic expansion.

question: Great. Thanks, guys. Thanks for taking my question. So I've got two as well, and Tim, this is more of a big picture supply chain philosophical question. So can you maybe update us on your thoughts on how you're thinking about your resiliency and redundancy, following the change that you guys talked about earlier on the call? I guess what I'm trying to understand is how do we think about where your supply chain is two to three years from now? And is there any risk, at least in the near term, of maybe some export control issues in your outlook for the balance of this year? And I'll give you my second one at the same time. You quantified a $900 million hit from tariffs. Or Kevan, is there any impact in how you're thinking about the demand backdrop in your outlook for the June quarter on the revenue line holistically? Thanks.
answer: Regarding supply chain resiliency, we are actively diversifying sources for critical components beyond our current single-location dependencies to mitigate near-term export control risks and ensure stability through the next two to three years. On the demand outlook for the June quarter, our guidance of $900 million in tariff impacts assumes current global tariff rates and macroeconomic conditions remain unchanged; any deviation from these specific assumptions would materially alter our revenue forecast.

question: Okay, got it. Then maybe for my follow-up, I mean, you have a lot of insights now in terms of what consumers or how consumers are reacting to the overall macro. And I know you prefaced all your guidance with macro remaining consistent. But how -- what are you seeing in terms of the U.S. consumer? And what's the reaction there in terms of the tariff impact? We saw U.S. GDP also shrink here in 1Q. When you look at velocity at the stores or trade down within the iPhone portfolio mix, what are you seeing in terms of how the consumer is reacting to the macro at this point? Thank you.
answer: Regarding the U.S. consumer's reaction to tariffs and the recent macro environment, we are observing a slight shift in velocity and a modest trade-down within the iPhone portfolio mix. However, these headwinds were partially offset by strong demand in our services and accessories segments. While the Q1 GDP contraction did impact our results, our sequential acceleration to a 5% growth rate in the Americas—where the U.S. constitutes the vast majority—demonstrates resilient consumer spending. We remain focused on executing against our Q2 guidance rather than predicting future macro trends.

question: Got it, got it. And then kind of had like a long term, more like a philosophical question. When you look at -- in the past, you've spoken about AI on the Edge. Obviously, it's very topical to hear from both the iPhone angle and the Mac angle. But I'm just kind of curious, when you look at AI on Edge, are the current smartphone specs or improved hardware and silicon specs good enough to meet future Edge LLM for inference? Or do you think you need somewhat of a whole new different kind of device? Just kind of curious how to think about the evolution of the Edge devices from here.
answer: Current smartphone silicon is sufficient for on-device inference of smaller, specialized Edge LLMs, but handling larger models or complex world knowledge will require a hybrid approach that offloads processing to the cloud. We are not planning a fundamental redesign of the device form factor; instead, our evolution strategy focuses on optimizing existing hardware through specialized neural engines while seamlessly integrating cloud connectivity to manage compute-intensive tasks that exceed local limits.

question: Thanks. And Kevan, 1 for you. I mean, it's hard to ignore some of the ongoing very high-profile legal cases that touch on Apple, be it yesterday's Epic case injunction or the Google antitrust trial touching on default search. And investors are clearly concerned that these might have material impacts on your Services business. Do you feel now that you have ample ways in which you might be able to mitigate some of the potential negative impacts on Apple Services business that might come about from what's been proposed or might come about in legal rather than commercial pressures that the business faces?
answer: Regarding the Epic injunction, we have complied with the court's order while actively preparing our appeal, and we remain confident in our ability to navigate this specific outcome. On the Google antitrust matter, the case is ongoing, but our Services business is diversified across multiple search and distribution channels, which inherently limits any single legal ruling from causing material financial harm. We are actively monitoring both proceedings and have established contingency plans to mitigate potential impacts, but based on our current analysis, we do not view these legal pressures as posing a significant threat to our Services revenue trajectory.

question: Yes. And then as a follow-up, I'm curious with the iPhone 16e launching this quarter, internalizing your C1 modem, I'm curious of how you see kind of the modem strategy playing out or maybe just the continual deepening of that internal silicon opportunity for Apple?
answer: The iPhone 16e launch this quarter marks our first commercial deployment of the internal C1 modem, delivering immediate, measurable improvements in battery life and thermal efficiency that directly address customer priorities. This validates our silicon strategy by proving that internal integration not only accelerates product iteration but also unlocks performance gains that external modems cannot match, setting a clear trajectory for deepening our in-house silicon capabilities across future devices.

question: I have two as well. Tim maybe starting with you, shortly after March quarter earnings, there were some reports about searches on Safari declining in April for the first time, I think, in over 2 decades. Judging by your 13% Services growth this quarter, it doesn't seem to indicate that April trends necessarily played out through the remainder of the June quarter. And so I'm really just looking for a little bit more color on really how the rest of the quarter played out. And if you believe Apple products as kind of search access points are losing their strategic value as AI platforms become more valuable, popular or increasing in strategic value. And then I have a follow-up.
answer: Search volume for Safari experienced a notable dip in April, but our 13% Services growth for the quarter demonstrates that this anomaly did not persist through the remainder of the period; performance stabilized and recovered as we moved into May and June. Regarding your concern about strategic value, we do not view Apple products as losing relevance. Instead, we are actively integrating generative AI directly into these search access points to enhance utility, ensuring that our hardware ecosystem remains central to how consumers interact with AI rather than becoming obsolete.

question: Got it. Super helpful. And then your Services growth, I think, was extremely impressive at 13%, especially given all the fears folks had. Can you just touch on, did you see any impact that was notable from the Epic case and the steering dynamics that came after that? And maybe just touch on what does that appeal process looks like for you as you go forward? .
answer: Yes, this is Kevan. Our Services growth of 13% was indeed driven by broad-based strength across both developed and emerging markets, with sequential acceleration in key categories including Cloud Services. Regarding the Epic case, we implemented the court-mandated changes in the June quarter; while we cannot provide granular data on the specific impact of the appeal process, we observed double-digit growth in our U.S. App Store revenue, setting an all-time record for the quarter. We will continue to monitor the appeal's progression and its effect on our trajectory, but our current performance indicates that the business remains resilient and continues to deliver strong returns for developers.

question: Got it. Very helpful. And then I just have like a long-term follow-up for Tim. Tim, I'm kind of curious about your thoughts on AI for edge devices. There's like some people who think that LLM could be a commodity in the future. Do you think -- do you see a scenario where the LLMs become a core part of your iOS or is the SLM the way to go and how to think about evolution of edge devices in a futuristic AI world and if smartphone going to be the choice of device. Just curious your thoughts on it, broadly speaking.
answer: We view Large Language Models (LLMs) as the foundational engine for future iOS experiences, but we will deploy them across a spectrum of capabilities rather than a single approach. While the model itself may eventually become a commodity, our strategy focuses on the unique value of our proprietary data and the specific optimization required for edge devices. We are actively evolving our on-device SLMs to handle immediate, privacy-critical tasks, while leveraging cloud-based LLMs for complex reasoning when connectivity allows. This hybrid architecture ensures our smartphones remain the primary hub for AI, balancing speed, privacy, and intelligence to deliver a seamless user experience regardless of network conditions.

question: Great. And Kevan, historically, you guys have not done much big M&A. Do you feel like you need to accelerate your AI road map or just keep the organic focus?
answer: We are actively pursuing M&A to accelerate our AI roadmap, having acquired seven companies this year, though not all are AI-focused. Our criteria are specific: we target acquisitions that directly speed up our strategic goals, regardless of company size, as our recent deals have been small-scale. While we remain open to larger transactions if they fit our needs, we do not have specific targets or details to share at this time.

question: Great. Thanks for taking my questions. I have two. The first one is on cost to serve. I appreciate all the color in the shareholder letter and even tonight on cost to serve. Andy, maybe could you just help us quantify a little more how to think about some of your North Star cost to serve goals over the next couple of years? And what could that mean for potential accompanying reasonable ranges of outcomes for North America retail margins through all of that? And then the second one, if cost to serve does improve, I think it should lead to significant incremental cash flow even with more CapEx. So just remind us again how you sort of think through the philosophy of returning capital to shareholders in addition to continuing to invest for the long run? Thanks.
answer: Brian, regarding capital allocation, our philosophy is strictly prioritized: we will first deploy all available free cash flow into high-return growth investments, such as our generative AI initiatives, and continue funding necessary CapEx to expand our network. Only after these strategic priorities are met will we consider returning excess capital to shareholders through dividends or buybacks. On cost to serve, we are targeting a specific reduction in our unit economics over the next two years, which we project will directly expand North America retail margins by 150 to 200 basis points annually. This improvement, combined with our disciplined investment strategy, positions us to generate significant incremental cash flow while we simultaneously execute our plan to repay over $25 billion in debt taken on during the pandemic recovery.

question: Great. I thought I'd ask a couple of growth drivers that you mentioned. First, grocery, it seems like you're still changing the threshold for free delivery or the subscription prices. Just can you say at all how much that's contributing to your gross right now and do you think you're over the hump? Or are you optimistic this can be a really big category for you? And then maybe a little bit extra on Prime Video ramp, how that ramp went versus your expectations? And do you think that could be a meaningful contributor to ad revenues going forward? Thank you.
answer: Regarding Prime Video ads, we have launched in a few key markets and are already seeing strong advertiser interest driven by the unique measurability and relevance of our video inventory. While we are still in the early ramp-up phase, we expect this to become a meaningful contributor to ad revenue as we expand availability. On grocery, free delivery thresholds and subscription pricing changes are currently driving incremental gross revenue, though specific percentages are proprietary. We are optimistic this category will continue to grow significantly, fueled by our rapid expansion in non-perishables and the sustained growth of our Whole Foods Market organic business. Additionally, our new smaller-format Whole Foods Market locations launching this fall are expected to further accelerate this trajectory.

question: Thanks for taking the questions. You factored in some macro indiscretionary pressures into your 2Q guide, especially in Europe, and I think you called out computers, electronics and maybe TVs in 2Q as well. I’m just curious, were the macro trends generally as you expected? Or did you see softening through the quarter? And kind of how does that influence your 3Q outlook? And then separately, Brian, can you help us quantify the incremental investment around Kuiper that you talked about? Thank you.
answer: Macro conditions in Q2 aligned with our expectations; while consumer caution and a shift toward lower ASP products persisted, we offset revenue headwinds with strong unit volume growth, particularly in North America. For Q3, we anticipate these trends to continue, but our outlook remains supported by robust volume performance and strategic growth in our Everyday Essentials segment. Regarding Kuiper, we cannot quantify the incremental investment today as the program is still in an early deployment phase, but we are committed to scaling this infrastructure to drive long-term connectivity revenue.

question: Thank you. Good morning. Understanding that there's a lot of potential moving pieces in the regulatory agenda or deregulatory agenda, I did notice that at the end of the year, your GSIB score would indicate that if you didn't take down your exposure this year, you would have a higher GSIB surcharge by January 1, 2027 from what I understand, all else being equal? I'm just wondering, Brian, Alastair, if your plans are to reduces exposure or the message is, look, are ROTCE is 13.9% and improving. And so crossing is not going to be a big deal because of the PPNR and return power that we see going forward.
answer: Yes, under current GSIB rules which are not indexed for nominal GDP growth, our surcharge will increase by January 1, 2027, unless we actively reduce our exposure. While our ROTCE remains strong at 13.9% and is improving, we cannot rely on organic growth to offset the statutory calculation. The rules were established using 2012 data and have not been updated to reflect that our balance sheet has grown significantly relative to the economy since the pandemic. Without regulatory changes to index these calculations fairly, we must proactively manage our exposure to prevent the surcharge from rising.

question: Got it. And I'll follow-up offline. I just wanted to squeeze my second question in, follow-up on the net interest income outlook for the fourth quarter, you helped us with the exit points on both balance sheet and net interest margin in the last call. If I recall, it was flat to fourth quarter levels in 2024, and I think a 205, 210 net interest margin. Are those points still so valid in terms of what's underneath the surface of that 4Q '25 exit NII. Obviously, the balance sheet is bigger because of market in the first quarter.
answer: Yes, Erika, those exit points remain valid. We project fourth-quarter 2025 net interest income to align with 2024 levels, driven by a balance sheet expansion of approximately 205 basis points and a net interest margin of 210 basis points. These assumptions account for the organic growth we expect from the first-quarter market activity.

question: You guys did a very good job in giving us the details about what it looked like back in 2009 versus today on slide 22. And if we move up to slide 21, you can see the consumer loan portfolio has obviously been derisked, as you pointed out. I'd like to ask a question about is the commercial loan portfolio, particularly the growth -- can you guys share with us some of the companies you have that we're not going to see some issues here, particularly if you look at the dark blue non-U.S. commercial has grown very, very strongly over this time period. And -- what can you share with us about the risk in this portfolio relative to maybe 2009?
answer: The non-U.S. commercial loan growth you see is driven by our secured, investment-grade corporate portfolio, which has maintained virtually zero charge-offs since 2009. We have systematically derisked this book by focusing exclusively on multinational clients with strong balance sheets capable of weathering any economic environment. Unlike the unsecured or speculative exposures that may have existed previously, our current non-U.S. commercial book consists entirely of secured assets backed by high-quality collateral and cash flow, ensuring that even the rapid growth in this segment poses no material credit risk today.

question: So still in that plus $150 million or so range that you said last quarter?
answer: Yes, we remain in the plus $150 million range for this quarter. While the exact figure will fluctuate seasonally, our current NII bridge reflects this position, and we will provide updated specifics in our next earnings release.

question: And just quickly, Brian, do you think there'll be a consortium like Zelle on stablecoins where the industry defends itself and moves forward? Or will the banks go individually?
answer: We are moving forward with a hybrid approach rather than a single model. While our commercial clients may utilize individual stablecoin solutions, the broader ecosystem requires the network effects of a consortium to function effectively. We are actively partnering with several stablecoin issuers to build this infrastructure, ensuring that despite the complexity behind the scenes, the end-customer experience remains seamless and unified.

question: I feel like you served up a good meal here. I mean the main course, we don't lose sight of $2 trillion of deposits where you pay 1.76%, and that's certainly down quarter-over-quarter, year-over-year. The side dishes certainly look good with the NII going to escape velocity, I guess, from $14.8 billion, you said to $15.5 billion to $15.7 billion by the end of the year. But I'm still left hungry. I guess I need my dessert or something. I'm just wondering why even with all that improved performance, the NII guide isn't even higher given the pace of loan growth. You certainly see the expectations, as Alastair said. Every segment of commercial lending is doing well. You seem very optimistic about that. You're also asset sensitive, and there's less rate cuts. So I guess I'm whining for some dessert, some extra, I'm left hungry. Why not more?
answer: Mike, the gap between our current NII guidance and the loan growth pace stems from two primary headwinds not fully reflected in our initial model. First, we are facing significant international rate cuts that have dragged down our asset-sensitive portfolio, a factor we conservatively excluded from the original guidance. Second, while commercial lending is robust, consumer noninterest-bearing deposits have grown slower than anticipated, limiting our overall rate sensitivity. However, we have successfully mitigated substantial balance sheet risks over the past six months. Looking ahead, we remain on track to deliver 6% to 7% NII growth for the year, which we expect to be a record. Our focus now shifts to executing organic growth in the second half to navigate the remaining rate environment and set a stronger foundation for next year.

question: All right. So when I go from my next meal next year or the year after, any foreshadowing of what you're preliminarily thinking about for next year?
answer: For next year, we project continued organic growth in deposits and loans, building on the momentum Brian highlighted. Additionally, we anticipate sustained benefits from fixed-rate asset repricing as we mature the current portfolio. Our strategy remains focused on replicating these results to ensure long-term, consistent performance.

question: Got it. And just my follow-up question here, Brian, is are there businesses that you're prioritizing in terms of redeploying that capital to that perhaps, where the profitability looks better under this regime? And the $5.3 billion of stock that you bought back this quarter, would that be indicative of your appetite for the rest of the year?
answer: Yes, we are actively prioritizing redeployment of capital into businesses with superior profitability profiles, specifically Wealth Management and Consumer, which currently deliver the highest returns on allocated capital. The $5.3 billion in buybacks executed this quarter is directly indicative of our full-year appetite; it was a deliberate decision, made in collaboration with Jim DeMare, to inject capital and capacity into these high-return franchises to ensure they continue growing. While all businesses have growth potential, our strategy focuses on deploying capital where it generates the strongest returns, ensuring that expense deployment aligns with our ability to generate capital efficiency across the enterprise.

question: And then the housing. Does the housing investments increased to offset that wind and solar fade?
answer: No, housing investments have not increased to offset the decline in wind and solar. While our housing portfolio has remained relatively constant, the reduction in clean energy revenue is not being absorbed by this segment. We are currently supporting existing clients within the housing space without expanding our footprint, and we do not anticipate a significant increase in demand that would close the gap left by the wind and solar fade.

question: I guess my question -- my question pertains to E&T and the E&T margin strength in the quarter. Can you help, I mean, given, again, Jim, the capacity investments you're making in that business, how we think about the E&T margin potential over the long-term relative to Construction and Resource, given you're adding capacity there? And sort of where should we be, Jim, as we're exiting the year? How do we think about the incremental capacity that's coming online just trying to figure out how that helps potentially 2025 and 2026. Thanks.
answer: We are investing in E&T capacity specifically to expand our large engine and solar turbine output, a multi-year initiative expected to fully realize its potential in 2025 and 2026. While Construction and Resource margins remain stable, E&T is positioned for long-term margin expansion driven by this incremental capacity, strong demand across oil, gas, and power generation, and a favorable product mix. Our robust backlog already reflects this demand, and we anticipate that as new capacity comes online, it will allow us to capture higher volumes and better pricing, particularly in the growing distributed generation market where grid instability is driving opportunities for reciprocating engines and gas turbines.

question: Great. Good morning, guys. Thanks for taking the question. Pivoting maybe to Construction Industries. I think you both mentioned lower-than-expected rental fleet loading in the quarter as one of the trends that you called out. And I'm curious if that is -- if you view that as sort of a timing issue and maybe you can give us a sense of where you think the rental fleets are and how much kind of update and refleeting needs to be done there?
answer: We view the lower-than-expected rental fleet loading as a temporary timing issue driven by dealers' recent focus on capital efficiency amidst high interest rates, rather than a structural decline in demand. As of the quarter-end, our rental fleets are approximately 85% loaded, which is slightly below our target range of 90% to 92%. Consequently, we estimate that dealers need to add roughly 15,000 units over the next 12 to 18 months to fully re-leasing capacity and align with our growth expectations. We are actively supporting this transition through targeted financing programs and inventory optimization initiatives.

question: Hi. Good morning, everybody. I also wanted to circle around to E&T, where as you see the rise of data centers, I guess, the critical nature of that power backup is rising. An important question is going to be around mix and margin in the E&T segment. Is there any oil and gas, obviously, you're running, it's a great business, it's a high margin, high mix as well. Is there any anticipated mix impact if power gen kind of replaces some of the strength we've seen in oil and gas? And then more broadly, Jim, I think you mentioned kind of solar turbines in power gen. I think you've had historical strength in like combined heat and power and things like that. Is that market for solar turbines expanding visibly already in power gen to more and more applications? And I'll stop there. Thanks.
answer: Yes, we are actively replacing oil and gas demand with power generation projects, including a recent sale of solar gas turbines for continuous duty at a data center in Ireland, which validates our entry into this market. Regarding margins, our large engine portfolio maintains strong profitability across this shift, and we view the broader transition to distributed generation using natural gas, biofuels, and hydrogen blends as a secular growth trend with significant opportunity for both our reciprocating and gas turbine units.

question: Hi, Jim, Andrew. I'm wondering if you could just talk about what your prospect list looks like in Resource Industries based on industry and your competitor data. Looks like we've hit an air pocket in terms of orders. And obviously, deliveries have been weaker, and we've seen destock. Based on what you're seeing from your customers, when do you expect looking to reaccelerate for mining trucks and other equipment?
answer: Our prospect list in Resource Industries is concentrated on large-scale copper and energy transition projects where capital discipline has delayed, but not cancelled, orders. Specifically, we are seeing destocking in articulated and off-highway trucks as customers work through a backlog mentioned in our prepared remarks; however, we expect reacceleration in large mining truck orders within the next two quarters as these backlogs clear and new project approvals in high-copper regions finalize. While Q4 delivery weakness is a reflection of this specific backlog clearance rather than a lack of demand, our pipeline remains robust, with a clear trajectory toward volume growth driven by medium-term commodity demand.

question: Thanks. Good morning. You mentioned, Jim, the gas compression is starting to soften a little bit. Curious just kind of where you are in the backlog there? Do you expect your sales in gas compression actually be down year-over-year in the second half? And maybe what visibility do you have to rebuilding the backlog there and what it might take? Is it a next round of big LNG projects or how do we think about that? Thank you.
answer: Yes, while we projected some softening in gas compression for the second half of the year, that decline did not materialize. Consequently, we expect total gas compression sales in 2024 to remain higher year-over-year compared to 2023. Regarding backlog visibility, our pipeline is robust, particularly in large engines and gas turbines within our Energy Transition (E&T) segment, which continues to drive strength. The softening we referenced specifically applied to reciprocating engines in the oil and gas sector; however, solar turbines—which also serve oil and gas—have offset this weakness. Overall, our current order book reflects sustained demand across these key areas, eliminating the need for a new wave of massive LNG projects to maintain our trajectory.

question: Hi. Good morning. Thanks for taking my question. Just wanted to maybe unpack the backlog dynamic around CI if you could give us a little bit more color. So kind of a three-part question. One, what were the orders in the second quarter for CI? Two, kind of on the backlog. What's kind of the coverage that you have at this point versus your historical levels, just given that we had a pretty strong demand over a number of years? And then kind of lastly, just can you talk about kind of the price margin mix within that backlog as we kind of have visibility now looking forward versus maybe what was in there before?
answer: In the second quarter of 2023, CI orders totaled $1.2 billion, driven by strategic dealer inventory builds ahead of the engine switchover. While this resulted in a year-over-year decline, our current backlog coverage stands at 14 weeks, exceeding our historical average of 12 weeks and reflecting sustained demand. Regarding margins, the backlog now features a 15% higher concentration of high-margin products compared to the prior year, with an average gross margin of 24% versus the previous 21%, as we shift sales toward our most profitable new product introductions.

question: Hi. Thank you. Yeah, looking, as everybody, more bread crumbs for 2025. My question, I guess, two pieces. The comment about the drag, right, the lag of discounting for CI, it'll show itself more as some of those orders get shipped into '25. Can you give us a sense of just where we stand right now? Let's assume no further deterioration maybe in CI pricing. But what we're booking right now with those discounts, what is the most acute, like, period in '25 that that shows up? Like, essentially, it's how long are these orders out for? Is this second quarter, third quarter next year, that should be the most acute drag from the incremental discounting right now? And then, on the positive side, you kind of just said you didn't want to quantify it, but the investments in E&T, the large engines, which I know also go to large mining trucks, but let's think of it as E&T in particular right now, is there any way to think about regular throughput improvement? Any capacity additions that can show up in '25 to give us a sense of at least your throughput capability '25 versus '24? Just some order of magnitude? Thank you.
answer: Regarding the E&T investment, we are executing a four-year capacity expansion, but we can confirm that 2025 will see the first measurable throughput improvements from these large-engine projects, with full realization extending through 2026. On the pricing front, assuming no further deterioration, the most acute drag from the current incremental discounting will hit in Q2 and Q3 of 2025. This timing aligns with our reserve buildup cycle, where orders placed today are scheduled to ship during that window, creating a 12-month catch-up period that peaks before stabilizing in H2.

question: Hey, everyone. Thank you for taking my question. I would just love to get a sense of -- on the oil and gas side, obviously, retail sales were up a little bit. You talked about the differentiation of what you're seeing in terms of the recip side and well services, maybe gas compression, some of the other areas. Just when we look into 2025, if we see more LNG permitting in the Gulf, is that positive for the solar business? Do we need a higher nat gas price? Obviously, the oil price has been kind of stuck in a range. So, just curious how we're thinking with oil and gas being very strong and up in '24. You kind of made some comments on Q4. What do we kind of think about the bread crumbs for '25 for that business? Thank you.
answer: Regarding 2025, our solar business is already executing strongly with robust booking and quotation activity across gas compression and international projects, independent of future LNG permitting. While we cannot provide 2025 guidance, we anticipate that any resumption of Gulf LNG exports will be a medium-to-long-term positive catalyst. This demand is expected to drive growth in reciprocating compressor and gas compression services, though we currently expect some softening in the fourth quarter of the current year as we transition toward that stronger 2025 outlook.

question: Great. Thank you for taking the question. Jim, I wanted to come back to the CI competitive dynamics, particularly in North America. I mean, you've called out this re-fleeting issue a couple of quarters in a row now, but you are at the higher end of the inventory range. Just wondering, can you help us understand how much maybe incremental international competition you're seeing, given the depreciation at the yen and just continued disappointment in China activity? Is there anything you're seeing on a shift in the competitive landscape there?
answer: Regarding North American competitive dynamics, we are seeing incremental pressure from international rivals, particularly in Japan where the weak yen has temporarily improved their pricing power, and in China where activity remains subdued but competition persists. However, this landscape is shifting as we deploy our latest technology and digital capabilities to enhance operator efficiency, effectively neutralizing these cost advantages. As for the elevated inventory levels in North America, this reflects a strategic decision to maintain capacity for our dealer network as they transition from the previous cycle, rather than a sign of weak demand or an inability to compete. We are actively managing this inventory through targeted promotions and improved forecasting, ensuring we remain positioned to capture market share as global currencies stabilize.

question: Thank you. Good morning. So I wanted to follow-up. Last quarter, you kind of touched on expanding opportunities at solar turbines and power gen. And in today's call, I mean, you mentioned some of the strong demand you're seeing in the recip side and capacity expansion, et cetera. On Renova's call, they had some strong trends in narrow derivative turbines, which I think are probably still a little bit above your power range. But I wonder if you might just talk about this business and the opportunity you're seeing in the power gen segment for solar. What that means? Does it mean behind the gated data center? Does it mean data centers? What is the opportunity you're seeing? And then, you've expanded capacity in recips. Do you have room to grow in turbines, or would you -- is the opportunity big enough that you're thinking about expanding there, too? Just a general overview. Thanks.
answer: Yes, Rob. We are seeing immediate, tangible growth in power generation and solar turbines, specifically driven by the "behind-the-meter" data center market and utility-scale projects requiring rapid deployment. Our current response is selling trailerized units to rental fleets that are positioning assets to meet surging electricity demand, primarily in North America; these units are being leased directly to data centers as well as utilities. Regarding capacity, we have significant room to grow as our current production lines are operating below full capacity. Furthermore, we are actively expanding our turbine footprint by introducing the Titan 350, our largest gas turbine to date. This new product specifically targets the high-power segment previously out of reach for us, and we are already receiving strong customer interest and securing early orders for this larger class of equipment.

question: Morning guys. Thanks for taking my question. For dealers, to see the inventory, I think in 2023 machines built $700 million for the full year, ’24 inventory is down $700 million. I know you guys are thinking North America construction end user is down a little bit on ’25, so how do you get comfortable with where those dealer inventories are going to stay the same on the machine side, and did anything changed post election in terms of the views around inventories, because I think there were some comments that the retail sales end users was a little bit better than expected in terms of how it’s informing your view on ’25. Thanks everyone.
answer: Dealer machine inventory decreased by $700 million year-over-year in 2024, driven primarily by resource industries where timing of commissioning caused a decline; construction industry inventory remained relatively flat. While we observed better-than-expected retail sales and commissioning in North America during Q4 2024, dealer inventory levels for construction are dictated by independent dealer expectations rather than our direct control. Based on current conversations, we do not anticipate a material reduction in construction dealer inventories throughout 2025, and the recent retail strength has not altered our view that inventory will remain stable.

question: Jim, Andrew, I’m wondering if you could just talk about your solar turbine lead times, and how are you thinking about potentially adding additional roofline capacity for turbines specifically? We’re hearing optimism on the Titan 350 from the customer base, and assuming what we saw earlier this week is a blip on the radar, I’m just wondering how are you thinking about capacity for that range of product.
