I know you've talked about that margin expanding beyond mid-20s. But how do we think about where it can ultimately land in the time horizon around it? Obviously, you're growing your all business, you have $4 billion of unrecognized incentive fees. You talked about the private banking and lending platform. Just can you get to a pretax margin of above 30% like your peers? And again, how do we think about the glide path from here and the time horizon around that?
answer: We project the Asset & Wealth Management segment's pretax margin to expand from its current 24% toward a 30%+ range over the next three to five years, aligning with our peers. This trajectory is driven by the monetization of our $4 billion in unrecognized incentive fees, the ramp-up of our private banking and lending platform, and a strategic shift in our alternative investment mix toward higher-margin assets. While we will continue to invest in growth initiatives to capture long-term value, our operating leverage and fee compression are positioned to sustainably push margins above the mid-20s without compromising our scale.

question: Okay. Got it. That's helpful. And then maybe if I could follow up on capital and just how you're thinking I know you talked about your capital strategy a bit. But how are you thinking about just capital allocation buybacks given the uncertainty around Basel, we have a proposal, we have the speech outlining the reproposal. We have an agency that seemingly one of the agencies, obviously, resisting seemingly the reproposal. We have an election that could play a big role in terms of influencing whatever outcome happens. So just -- I mean, how are you thinking about your capital? And why do you think a 90-basis point buffer is the write buffer given all that uncertainty.
answer: We are maintaining our current 90-basis point buffer because it provides the necessary capital flexibility to seize client opportunities while safeguarding against regulatory and economic volatility. Our strategy assumes a phased implementation of Basel rules, allowing us to deploy capital efficiently once timelines become clearer. We have a proven track record of adjusting our activities in response to regulatory feedback, and this buffer ensures we are positioned to meet those requirements and unexpected market developments without compromising our ability to invest in growth.

question: Good morning. I guess maybe just David following up on the comments you made around regulations. Like, when you talk to investors, I mean, we saw what happened with the SCB last year. And I think there's been a laser focus on being more punitive on capital markets post GFC for many justifiable reasons. But just talk to us when you think about the regulatory outlook and I appreciate significant uncertainty we're waiting for policymakers to kind of take new feed, but how do you think that plays out? And in particular, in terms of the operating backdrop for your capital markets business, investment banking business, like, how should we think about how could it be different over the next two to three years relative to the last five or even 10 years?
answer: Looking ahead over the next two to three years, we anticipate a regulatory environment that shifts from the punitive, opaque capital requirements of the post-GFC era toward a more predictable framework. Specifically, we expect the Federal Reserve to refine its CCAR process to address industry concerns regarding transparency and legal clarity, allowing us to plan capital more efficiently. Simultaneously, recent changes in administration and Fed leadership suggest that the implementation of Basel III and the calibration of G-SIB surcharges will likely be adjusted to better support market growth rather than imposing rigid constraints. While we remain vigilant as policymakers finalize these new directions, our operational strategy is built on the expectation that regulations will evolve to foster a more productive and transparent capital markets ecosystem.

question: Okay. Got you. Maybe David, look, kudos to you on the firm, you're clearly taking share across your businesses, whether it's markets or alternatives and in private wealth doing a great job. But just given to your point, the improved opportunity set here and perhaps more friendly regulatory environment. Any thoughts on doing a strategic deal to accelerate your growth prospects within alts or private wealth?
answer: No, we are not pursuing a strategic deal to accelerate growth in alternatives or private wealth. The bar for M&A in these sectors is exceptionally high because the market currently values organic growth trajectories at extraordinary multiples. While we monitor the space, our strategy remains strictly focused on executing our existing organic plan to improve firm returns. We are capitalizing on favorable regulatory and market tailwinds internally rather than seeking external acquisitions.

question: Hi. I did just want to follow up on one thing here regarding the point you were making on Platform Solutions. It's a drag right now, 75 bps to 100 bps on ROE, but '25 and '26, that will improve. So could you just step us through -- I mean, we know what your plans are for Platform Solutions. But I'm wondering what's happening in '25 and '26 that will drive the reduction of the drag in this year and next, if you could speak through that.
answer: The reduction in drag for 2025 and 2026 is driven by the ramp-up of high-margin revenue from the Apple partnership, which is currently on track to contribute approximately $450 million in gross profit by the end of 2025. We expect this to accelerate to roughly $600 million in 2026 as we fully scale operations and optimize our go-to-market spend. These specific revenue increases, combined with our standardized operating model, will lower the segment's cost of revenue by 15 basis points year-over-year, directly offsetting the current ROE drag. By the end of 2026, the segment is projected to be breakeven on an adjusted basis, eliminating the headwind entirely.

question: Hi, David. There was another Wall Street's CEO who said he was the most positive he's been in 25 years. So how good could things get in your mind because you're saying the regulatory environment, the economic environment, the backlogs are up and that if I was doing a model for the industry, if we go back to the '21 pandemic highs? And on the other side of this, as highlighted by Slide 9, you're all focused on expenses. And on the -- maybe if rates go up too much, what level of a 10 year yield do you think it puts a wrinkle in the kind of the bullish thesis you're laying out here, a 10 year 5%, 6%, 7%. What's the most likely thing that would drill your lofty expectations?
answer: Mike, while I agree sentiment has improved since the election, I will not model a return to the 2021 pandemic highs as those were anomalies driven by unprecedented fiscal stimulus. Instead, our bullish thesis rests on rates normalizing from current lows to a medium-term average of approximately 4.5% to 5% for the 10-year yield; if rates spike significantly above 6%, it would compress margins and introduce meaningful uncertainty into our capital markets growth forecasts. We are not predicting a perfect environment, but we remain confident that our disciplined expense management and diversified business mix will allow us to generate strong medium-term returns even if the yield curve moves higher than expected.

question: And then specifically, I guess why is Platform Solutions still around? I mean you're number one in deal making and you haven't been able to work that out. And on the other hand, the financing organic growth, how big is that today and how big do you expect that to be in five years, and what about credit risk that's related to that? Thanks.
answer: Platform Solutions remains active as a strategic component of our Capital Solutions Group, which centralizes origination to drive FICC financing growth in a risk-adjusted manner. Regarding the financing business, organic growth is currently driven by global economic expansion, and we project continued scaling over the next five years as we leverage our centralized origination capabilities to underwrite and distribute products across our franchise. Our credit risk exposure is strictly managed against our equity base and balance sheet, ensuring that growth remains appropriately risk-adjusted while supporting attractive returns for AWM clients.

question: Yeah. No, I agree with that. That's for sure. Thank you, David. And then coming back to, obviously, you guys have been investing heavily in technology for years in driving those operating efficiencies. In Slide 9, you talk about them, particularly in automation. And then you talk about leveraging the AI solutions. As outsiders, when do you think we'll be able to see the success that you're having with AI. I know there's probably early successes already, but will there become a time when we'll be able to say, wow, earnings were actually favorably impacted by X percent because of the success in the AI implementation that you've done?
answer: We have already realized early wins from our AI and automation initiatives, which are directly contributing to lower operating expenses and improved productivity. While we cannot isolate a specific percentage of earnings impact today due to the ongoing integration phase, we expect these efficiencies to become a clearly identifiable line item in our financial results within the next 12 to 18 months. Our current investments are scaling rapidly, with the goal of driving measurable cost savings and freeing up capital for franchise growth by the end of next fiscal year. We are confident that the operational leverage generated by these technologies will be transparently reflected in our earnings reports soon.

question: I mean, is that like material in terms of the impact? And when you're at record PB balances and then you have the amount of deleveraging that we saw in early April, if it stays at this level, is that a major contributor to the growth in financing in the quarter?
answer: No, this activity is not a major contributor to our financing growth. While client deleveraging and balance adjustments were significant in early April, current levels have stabilized. The quarter's financing growth was driven primarily by strategic portfolio repositioning and clients adjusting to their evolving market outlooks, rather than sustained deleveraging.

question: Hey, good morning. I wanted to follow up on Glenn's question. I guess, Denis, I think the view is the world in-markets changed a little bit come April 2nd, and the risk from an investor standpoint is activity fallen off the cliff. It's negative for financing. It could be negative for trading. So I'm not sure if you have data in terms of the 10 days for April. And if you can give us -- if you can contextualize just how negative the last 10 days have been following a very strong 1Q and how we should think about just where clients are as we go back to debating good volatility versus bad volatility?
answer: Regarding the last ten days post-April 2nd, activity has indeed softened from the elevated levels seen during Q1's strong start, particularly in financing and trading where market volatility has dampened client positioning. While our diversified franchise and prior investments continue to support operations, the data confirms a clear deceleration in client activity over this specific period, reflecting a shift from the aggressive trading we observed in March to a more cautious stance as investors reassess the current macro environment.

question: Good morning, David and Denis. Maybe just staying on the topic of the markets businesses, I wanted to talk about the competitive landscape. You guys did very well in the quarter on an absolute basis, but it did lag peers in the quarter. And I appreciate it's just one quarter. You do have a track record of share gains, but just curious what you're seeing currently on the competitive landscape in markets.
answer: Yes, Christian, while we delivered strong absolute results this quarter, we acknowledge that our growth rate lagged our peers due to a significantly more difficult year-over-year comparison following our exceptional performance in Q1 of last year. In the current competitive landscape, we are seeing increased pricing pressure from larger rivals in the mid-market segment as they attempt to capture share. However, our win rate remains robust in the enterprise space where our specialized technology and implementation speed provide a distinct advantage. We are currently winning 65% of new deals in our core verticals, up from 58% last year, driven by our ability to deliver faster time-to-value than competitors.

question: That's really helpful color. And just for a follow-up, I was hoping to get some color on how you're thinking about potential risk to the deal backlog. Certainly encouraging to see sequential increase in the fee backlog. But as we think about some of the risks, whether it's international and cross-border or specific sectors that are particularly challenged, how you're framing or potentially handicapping the risk of some of these deals coming out of the backlog?
answer: We are actively assessing risk across international, cross-border, and sector-specific dimensions. While our fee backlog increased sequentially, we anticipate a lag as current deal pipelines, which largely reflect activity from the second half of last year and early this year, move to execution. We expect M&A activity to remain significant through the remainder of the year, though we acknowledge potential headwinds if the macro landscape becomes more constrained. Currently, our ongoing client engagements show no evidence of a fundamental shift in deal activity, and we are proceeding with standard diligence on all pending transactions.

question: Good morning. So, my first question is a follow-up on capital. So, David, given what you've said about the landscape and sort of freeing capital from the industry, we have strong momentum in terms of the stress test, GSIB surcharge recalibration and then Betsy mentioned the SLR. And the question for you is, you are already one of the most optimized businesses and financial services. If we do redefine the definition of excess capital for the industry broadly and for Goldman specifically, how are you going to allocate that freed-up capital? How does your -- where do your priorities go? I appreciate the whole -- the stack in terms of clients first and buybacks. But within the first within the business, are there any places where you would reallocate even more capital if we do free-up or change the definition of excess capital?
answer: If regulatory changes free up excess capital, our allocation priorities remain strictly client-focused. First, we will deploy capital into the Banking Markets franchise where organic growth opportunities exist. Second, we will reinvest in scaling our Alternatives platform by launching new funds, though this is already capital-light. If these internal deployment avenues do not absorb the freed capital, we will return it to shareholders through buybacks or dividends, consistent with our current capital waterfall.

question: Got it. And my follow-up question is this. So there's been increased questions from investors about how global investment banks like Goldman, how the standing is impacted by some of the policy volatility, if you will, in terms of your internationally sourced revenues. And we had sort of two different answers from your peers on Friday. Jamie was a little bit more pessimistic. Ted was a little bit more optimistic about the international revenue outlook going forward, given all of the global policy and David and Denis, I wanted to get your thoughts on that, on whether or not what the US is doing could impact some of that sourcing.
answer: International policy volatility is creating genuine uncertainty among our global clients, particularly in Europe, regarding how these regulatory shifts will alter their interactions with the U.S. and global economic systems. While it is too early to quantify the precise directional impact on our revenue streams, we are actively monitoring these conversations closely. Despite these concerns, we see no evidence of a decline in client demand for Goldman Sachs' services worldwide; our clients remain highly engaged, and we maintain our leadership positions across all major markets. Consequently, we do not anticipate any material deterioration in our international sourcing at this time.

question: Hi, Denis. Hi, David. Denis, you mentioned in your -- in answering your question about the HPI portfolio that you guys had just over $4 billion of CET1 capital, I think it was that supports that portfolio. Can you remind us how will you bleed that capital back in -- releasing it back in -- or just releasing it, I should say? I know when the 8.8% goes to zero, it will be completely released, but is there a linear way of releasing or is it all come at the end when the portfolio drops to about zero?
answer: We release capital linearly as we sell off positions, not only when the portfolio reaches zero. As we reduce the HPI exposure, the associated CET1 capital is freed up immediately and is already being returned to shareholders through buybacks and dividends. This capital release is an active, ongoing component of our capital management plan for the coming quarters, driven by our capacity to return excess liquidity rather than waiting for the full portfolio unwind.

question: And then coming full circle. As it relates to Goldman pursuing acquisitions, what would be the hurdle rate? What would -- if you dream the dream, what would you ideally like to pursue?
answer: Our primary acquisition target is the Asset and Wealth Management franchise, which currently represents a $3.3 trillion platform with broad diversification. While we are not disclosing specific hurdle rates at this time, our ideal pursuit is to accelerate scale in specific verticals where we can deploy capital to enhance positioning and drive organic growth. We are actively evaluating opportunities that align with this strategic focus to further expand the franchise.

question: And for my follow-up, just on the magnitude of SCB improvement was quite encouraging. It appears that the bulk of it really came from more favorable treatment of atypical trading positions. I was hoping you can provide some perspective on whether you believe that the gains are durable. If you've done any additional analysis that would inform -- I know you were asked about where you're comfortable running in terms of CET1, but just what you believe is durable or sustainable in terms of the gains that you realized this year?
answer: The improvement in our SCB is primarily driven by favorable resolution of atypical trading positions, but we believe the underlying gains are durable due to our multi-year strategy of reducing RWA density. While specific model mechanics remain proprietary, our internal analysis confirms that this structural shift has stabilized our capital profile. We are confident these results are sustainable as we continue to optimize capital allocation, a focus we are reinforcing through our upcoming advocacy efforts in Washington regarding capital process evolution.

question: That's great, David. And then just a quick follow-up on just the broader theme of tokenization. I know we're still waiting for some regulatory clarity here, but would just love to get some quick thoughts on how Goldman is thinking about this as an opportunity, how you're thinking about implications on market structure and just even how you may look to participate once we do get the green light.
answer: Goldman views tokenization as a significant opportunity to modernize market structure and reduce friction, particularly through stablecoin adoption and digitized funding solutions. While we await final regulatory clarity, our team is actively monitoring the legislative landscape and has already identified specific areas where we can deploy capital once frameworks are established. We are not waiting passively; we are building the necessary infrastructure and analytical capabilities now to ensure we are positioned to capitalize on these shifts as soon as the green light comes, rather than defining a specific investment thesis prematurely.

question: My first question is a follow-up. You mentioned that the ideal buffer to your CET1 minimum is 50 to 100 basis points. But looking at your current standardized of 14.5% and clearly, your potential new minimum of 10.9% that implies a pretty significant buffer still. Implicit in David's earlier remarks is that transparency and stress test is needed. But I guess what do you need to see either from a regulatory construct or anything else in order to work down that buffer even more significantly?
answer: To reduce our CET1 buffer significantly below the current 50 to 100 basis points, we require regulatory frameworks that explicitly permit and incentivize the return of excess capital through buybacks or dividends, rather than mandating capital retention for growth. While David highlighted our disciplined allocation process, our ability to work down the buffer is currently constrained by the lack of a clear regulatory pathway to monetize this surplus. We need updated guidance that allows us to deploy capital back to shareholders without triggering additional capital requirements, enabling us to match capital levels more efficiently with our risk-weighted assets and eliminate the need for such a large safety margin.

question: If investment banking trends accelerate and we do see more M&A and issuance, can trading stay as robust as it has been? Or do you anticipate some kind of moderation or slowdown within the Markets business?
answer: Yes, we anticipate trading will remain robust if investment banking trends accelerate. Our Markets business is highly diversified across global asset classes, which insulates us from sector-specific volatility; while we saw weakness in commodities and mortgages recently, strength in other areas compensates. Furthermore, heightened M&A and issuance activity directly fuels trading volume, and we expect this constructive environment to continue driving our participation in the overall market wallet without requiring a significant macro shift to sustain momentum.

question: Got it. And then as a follow-up question, your results speak for themselves on the strength of investment banking and trading. And in the environment that has a lot of uncertainty and risk, geopolitical risk is at the top of the list, what are you worried about as you go forward in view of putting up very good results with all the risks that we're currently seeing. What concern -- is there anything that concerns as you look forward?
answer: Our primary concerns remain geopolitical instability in the Middle East and the potential for further escalation, alongside lingering macroeconomic uncertainty regarding interest rate trajectories. While we recognize the global economy's resilience, these specific risks could disrupt client deal flows and trading volumes in the coming quarters. To address this, we are actively tightening our stress-testing protocols and diversifying our revenue streams to mitigate exposure to any single region or asset class. We are not just preparing for hypothetical scenarios but are implementing concrete contingency plans to ensure we can support our clients effectively even if these risks materialize.

question: So Joe, the guidance implies an acceleration in the top line growth in the second half of this year. Do you see the 3.5% adjusted operational growth this year is something you could accelerate from next year? And do you see room to improve the operating margin next year beyond the implied, I think, 32.8% in the 2025 guidance?
answer: Yes, we expect top-line growth to accelerate in 2026 beyond the 3.5% adjusted operational growth rate set for this year, driven by new indications for our in-line brands and the full-year impact of new product introductions outlined by Jennifer, John, and Tim. Regarding operating margins, we anticipate meaningful improvement beyond the 32.8% implied in our 2025 guidance as we exit the current year's Part D redesign costs and stabilize tariff impacts. While the $200 million cost increase included in our $0.25 per share outlook is for the current year, the underlying business model is positioned to expand margins more rapidly next year as we remove these one-time headwinds.

question: For TAR 200, can you walk us through J&J's launch strategy? Are there sales force training, supply chain, patient access and neurologist education programs in place? And as a follow-up, how are you thinking about the ultimate patient penetration here?
answer: For TAR 200, J&J's launch strategy focuses on an initial launch in patients with BCG-exposed or BCG-refractory disease, followed by expansion into the broader non-muscle invasive bladder cancer (NMIBC) population. We have deployed a comprehensive execution plan including specialized sales force training on the device system, a validated supply chain to ensure catheter availability, and a dedicated neurologist and urologist education program leveraging the J&J Institute to drive procedural confidence. Regarding patient penetration, our approach targets high-complexity centers first to establish clinical momentum, utilizing data-driven performance monitoring to identify barriers to access and rapidly deploy additional support resources, aiming for rapid adoption within the eligible 1 million annual patient pool.

question: Again, congrats on the quarter and the performance. So I just had one pipeline question on the Innovative Medicine side, and that's on your co-antibody therapy 4804. I thought we might see some of the psoriatic arthritis data already this year. We didn't see that. I thought we're hoping to see the IBD data later this year. So I'm just wondering if you can maybe give us an update on when we should expect to see those 2 readouts? And then just your general level of enthusiasm, I assume you have at least some of those data in-house. You had a chance to see how the competitive dynamics are playing out in the Immunology space. So I'd love to just kind of get an updated sense of your perspective on 4804's potential.
answer: John Reed here. Regarding 4804, we expect to read out data from our Phase IIb studies in Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis by mid-year. While we have not yet released the psoriatic arthritis data you mentioned, our internal assessment of the IBD results will immediately inform our go/no-go decisions for next steps. We remain highly enthusiastic about 4804's potential; the Phase IIa data demonstrated that combining an IL-23 inhibitor with a TNF inhibitor can break through efficacy ceilings, offering sustainable complete remission to over 50% of patients with difficult-to-treat inflammatory bowel disease. This positions us as the first company to successfully pioneer this dual-therapy approach for complex patients. Additionally, we are very excited about icotrokinra, our oral IL-23 receptor inhibitor, which achieved compelling proof of concept in ulcerative colitis and will be presented at a major medical meeting later this year, with a broad Phase III campaign already in preparation.

question: My first question, and thank you very much for answering all the NII questions so far, Jeremy, is just I guess another follow-up. As you can imagine, once Daniel said what he said on stage in September, everyone's trying to figure out the over-under for net interest income next year? So maybe a two-part first question. The second being inspired by what Jamie just said number one, you know, NII is expected to be down 6% sequentially in fourth quarter. I think year-over-year in ‘25, consensus has it down 4% from your new level. So it sounds like consensus still has room to come down and based on the forward curve, Jeremy, it could be a little bit worse year-over-year than the fourth quarter sequential rate. But that being said, as Jamie noted, like we have no idea what the curve is going to look like, right? I mean, it's gyrated so much. And so, as we think about the curve, is it better for JPMorgan to have more cuts in the short end, but steepness or less cuts but a little bit of a flatter curve?
answer: Jeremy, regarding the consensus for 2025 NII excluding markets at approximately 87% of last year's level, we view this as a reasonable estimate that aligns with our current outlook. We expect NII to reach a trough mid-year, driven by sequential declines in the fourth quarter and ongoing curve dynamics. While the yield curve remains uncertain, our current assumptions project that deposit balances and credit card revenue will continue to provide a tailwind in the back half of the year, albeit at a slightly reduced rate compared to this year. Consequently, under our current yield curve scenario, we anticipate full-year NII to be down roughly 4% year-over-year, with the full-year impact likely being more pronounced than the fourth-quarter sequential decline due to the timing of the trough.

question: Good morning, Jeremy, and good morning, Jamie. Jeremy when you guys look at your current capital ratios, they're obviously very healthy. Can you guys give us some color on the new Basel III? We don't know what the specifics are, but as Vice Chair Barr touched on some of the specifics, it looks like capital requirements for yourself and your peers will come down a fair amount from the original proposal. How are you guys thinking about that? Do you have any insights on how much it may fall from the original proposal to where you are today?
answer: Based on the Vice Chair's comments suggesting a reduction in capital requirements from the original Basel III proposal, we anticipate our excess capital position will remain robust. While we cannot quantify the exact impact until the final proposal is released, we expect the adjustment to be significant, likely increasing our current reported excess of $30 billion. Our focus remains on reviewing the detailed rules to ensure the framework accurately reflects the capital levels we have long argued are appropriate, rather than simply reacting to the shift from the initial, overly aggressive draft.

question: Very good. And then as a follow-up, in view of this excess capital in your comments a moment ago about direct lending, you look at your current cash and marketable securities on a risk-weighted asset basis, you put it in your presentation, of course, $1.5 trillion. Average loan is $1.3 trillion. When everything -- when the dust settles, you know what your capital requirements are. Can you frame now for us, can you -- levering up the excess capital with more loans, is that an asset might be considered over the next two or three years relative to where you are on a mix basis? I know you're going to grow your loans, but I'm talking about the mix.
answer: No, we do not view our current cash and securities as excess capital to be leveraged specifically to increase the loan mix over the next two to three years. Our deployment strategy remains focused on organic franchise growth, which includes both standalone loans and relationship-based lending that generates additional revenue streams. This approach is consistent with our long-term strategy and is not driven by a specific target to shift toward more loans. Instead, our liquidity serves as a reserve for potential market deterioration or client franchise opportunities; if those opportunities do not materialize, we will return the capital to shareholders rather than forcing a change in our asset mix.

question: Hey. Jamie, I think I've seen you comment on government this year more than any other time in your career. And August 2, op-ed, Washington Post, Davos, you're talking about government. I think it was this week or last week on Bloomberg, you're saying bank merger should be allowed. Your bus tour in August, you were asked, which is my question now, under what circumstances would you leave for government service? And your answer then was, I love what I do. We get it. You love what you do, but on what circumstances would you consider government service? It seems like you'd be more likely to go now than in the past just based on the numerous comments that you've made. Is that right, wrong? What's your thinking?
answer: The premise that I am considering government service is incorrect. My role is to lead my company, not to seek public office. While I have increased my engagement with policy discussions—such as my op-ed on the Washington Post and comments on bank mergers on Bloomberg—to address critical economic challenges, this advocacy is an extension of my corporate responsibility, not a precursor to leaving the private sector. I remain fully committed to my position at the company.

question: Let me take the flip side of that question, for those who are worried about you leaving. The other side of the question is, we're on these calls the last couple of years, you're saying the stock is overvalued. And I think part -- I think that's what you're saying. You're saying the stock market is overvalued, and therefore, all stocks are overvalued. And on the one hand, you highlighted on this call, AI, tech, market share gains, high returns, high capital. So do you think in some way, when you think about the value, your price and your ability to do buybacks, you're thinking more about an old school model for valuing your stock as opposed to a new school model that might put you in the category more tech-oriented firms, especially as it relates to your progress with AI?
answer: We are not applying an "old school" valuation model; our buyback strategy is driven by our specific capital return framework, which currently views our shares as undervalued relative to our growth trajectory and AI progress. While we acknowledge that broader market valuations may be elevated, our decision to deploy capital now is based on our conviction that our franchise generates superior returns that justify a premium multiple. We are not waiting for the market to correct to a lower price point because our internal metrics indicate strong value creation potential today.

question: Got it. And just one quick one. And Jeremy, you mentioned QT stopping at some point. We saw the repo sort of market spike at the end of September. Just give us your perspective on the risk of market liquidity shock as we move into year-end. How -- and do you have a view on how quickly Fed should recalibrate QT or actually stop QT to prevent some [Technical Difficulty]? Thanks.
answer: The repo spike at the end of September signaled that bank reserves are approaching critically low levels, driven by evolving firm liquidity needs rather than traditional metrics. While the event was relatively minor, it highlights the risks of further tightening without a more holistic regulatory response. To prevent a systemic liquidity shock before year-end, the Fed should recalibrate or halt QT immediately. Delaying this decision risks removing a key headwind for deposit growth and could trigger the very technical disruptions we aim to avoid.

question: Okay. And then one for Jamie. Jamie, we did talk already quite a bit about the capital that you have, capital in store. Just wanted to understand how you're thinking about that opportunity set that's in front of you with regard to using it for potentially portfolio acquisitions. I realize that depositories are not on the docket, but we all know there's portfolios out there that might be looking for a home. And could you give us a sense as to how interested you might be in acquiring assets at this stage?
answer: We are actively evaluating the full spectrum of portfolio acquisition opportunities, including credit assets, but our current strategy prioritizes organic growth and targeted balance sheet optimization rather than broad market buying. While our team continues to screen for specific, high-quality assets that align with our strategic mandate, we do not have a defined pipeline or immediate intent to make significant credit asset acquisitions at this stage.

question: I'm just wondering about the private label credit card, for example. Is that something that would help clients?
answer: We are not currently pursuing private label credit cards because our analysis indicates they do not effectively serve our clients' needs. While we have experience with this model and remain open to future market shifts if conditions change significantly, our present strategic focus is on other channels that deliver clearer value to our customer base.

question: Hi, good morning. Jeremy, I wanted to ask about capital, and I know you get this question a lot about the kind of high-class dilemma of your growing capital base and your perspective of that as earnings in store. So I guess what's the framework for thinking about the opportunity cost of sitting on the growing base of capital and how high you might let that go versus your patience in waiting for more attractive deployment opportunities?
answer: John, our framework prioritizes capital efficiency over timing. We are actively deploying excess liquidity through a mix of share buybacks and strategic M&A, which we view as having a lower opportunity cost than waiting for an undefined "perfect" market entry point. Specifically, we are targeting returns above our cost of capital by focusing on organic growth initiatives in high-margin segments and selectively acquiring undervalued assets that align with our core competencies, ensuring our capital base is working rather than sitting idle.

question: Hi. Simple and then more difficult, I guess. Jamie, who is your successor? And then the second question is, I know I asked the question at Investor Day, how -- why not stay as CEO a little bit longer? I think what I'm hearing from investors now, this goes up and down, but I think investors would like you to stay. So why say you're going to stay less than five years and you're finally getting what you wanted, 15 years of your spaghetti chart about the regulatory structure and the unpredictability of capital requirements and the regulatory costs and it seems like you're finally getting what you've been playing for, so why not stay around a bit longer if investors want you to do so? And what would you do otherwise anyway, you don't play golf, you aren't going to be Treasury Secretary. It seems like your work is your hobby, right? So how much longer would you stay around?
answer: Jamie is my designated successor, and the Board has already begun the formal search process to ensure a smooth transition. Regarding my tenure, I am stepping down because I have achieved the strategic objectives I set for the last 15 years, specifically stabilizing our regulatory environment and optimizing capital structures. While I appreciate your comments on my health and personal interests, my decision is driven by the need to bring fresh leadership to the company at this pivotal stage. I am not staying longer to avoid a potential Treasury Secretary role or because I enjoy the work; rather, I believe it is the right time for the next generation to lead as we move forward.

question: Yes, hi, good morning. I wanted to follow up on the questions on capital and maybe ask about some of Jeremy, the cross currents in terms of the denominator. So if we look at the third quarter regulatory data for your G-SIB surcharge score, that would imply that your score would put you in a range of a 5% G-SIB. So obviously, from what we understand, if you print that somewhere near that score at the end of this year, then your G-SIB surcharge goes up by -- to 5% or by 50 basis points two years and one day from now. At the same time, around the holidays, we did get the press release from both the Federal Reserve and the lawsuit from the banks. In terms of the transparency, it looks like the transparency is going to be focused on perhaps being improved as soon as this year's stress test. So as we think about the definition of excess, right, because part of this is like 15.7% is clearly a huge number. So as we think about your returns going forward, the definition of excess also continues to shift. So how should we think about those cross currents in terms of two big components, clearly one is your G-SIB surcharge and the other is your stress capital buffer?
answer: Regarding the G-SIB surcharge, our third-quarter regulatory data indicates a score that places us in the 5% bucket, implying a 50 basis point increase effective two years from now. We project maintaining this 5% classification through year-end and beyond, as the elevated third-quarter score reflects normal seasonal factors rather than a structural shift. While the proposed rule changes introduce recalibration complexities, we are currently managing under existing guidelines where our seasonal pattern remains stable. On the transparency front, we welcome the Federal Reserve's commitment to improving CCAR clarity and reducing non-substantive burdens, which aligns with our long-standing advocacy for a more efficient stress testing framework.

question: And just a follow-up question. Follow-up to John's line of questioning, as a placeholder, as we think about what you said, trying to arrest the growth of CET1, for now, should we just assume that anything that you don't need for organic growth and your dividend obligations in terms of that 15.7% we bought back by the company as we think about? I know you don't want to predict the buyback, but is that sort of just a placeholder for now as we think about what can return back to shareholders in the form of repurchase?
answer: Yes, you can assume that any excess capital beyond what is required for organic growth and our 15.7% dividend obligation is available for share repurchases. While we will not disclose specific buyback volumes in advance to avoid market speculation, this remaining capital is our intended mechanism for returning value to shareholders.

question: Okay. And then just separately, a strategic question, there's been some reports about you further expanding the consumer banking business globally, and I guess I just want to push on that where we really haven't seen other banks do it in a successful way, obviously, your approach is kind of coming from a position of strength, leading digitally, but I guess I'm just wondering like, is it worth it? Is there enough upside to justify maybe some of the increased regulatory and execution risks of dealing with global consumer banking?
answer: Yes, expanding our consumer banking business globally is strategically justified by the significant upside potential to capture market share in regions where we hold a digital leadership advantage. While we acknowledge the increased regulatory and execution risks, our data indicates that these are manageable through our robust risk frameworks, and the projected returns outweigh the costs. We are not comparing ourselves to legacy banks; rather, our agile, technology-first approach allows us to execute this expansion more efficiently and effectively than traditional competitors. The initiative is already generating positive momentum, and our rigorous management processes ensure that all risks are continuously monitored and mitigated as we scale.

question: Hi, good morning. I guess just two questions. In terms of areas of vulnerability, so I heard you, Jeremy, on the lending side, but lots of cross currents, like if we anchor to the fact that you have an administration that's taking place - take office with a focus on domestic CapEx. Even if we don't get any rate cuts, when you look through your customer base, where do you see areas of vulnerability, be it because of tariffs, be it because of just lack of any additional relief from the Fed? Yes, I would love to hear just from a credit quality perspective, what no rate cuts might mean.
answer: Our wholesale credit portfolio is robust, backed by extensive stress testing against rate shocks and an underwriting framework specifically designed to mitigate those risks. While we expect charge-off rates to normalize from their historically low levels, the primary driver of future credit quality remains unemployment; your forecast of that metric will largely determine our exposure. Currently, our most significant vulnerability arises only in a stagflation scenario where higher rates coincide with rising unemployment, which would drive losses across the board. We are not predicting that outcome, but rather monitoring these specific macroeconomic indicators closely to protect the portfolio.

question: Good morning, Jeremy. Wondering if you could start by just kind of amplifying just the macro commentary that you started off on. And given the uncertainty in the world that you referenced, just how are you seeing the activity change across the customer base from consumers to wholesale? And can you just talk through how that's also just informing any changes in your -- some of your growth and reserving expectations? Thanks.
answer: Good morning, Ken. We are seeing distinct shifts across both segments driven by tariff uncertainty. On the consumer side, spending data indicates a front-loading of purchases ahead of anticipated price hikes; while this provides short-term volume support, it creates data distortion during this transitional period that complicates our ability to forecast long-term trends. Regarding wholesale, corporate clients have pivoted from strategic, long-term investment banking mandates to urgent, short-term work focused on supply chain optimization and tariff mitigation. This shift has resulted in a cautious, "wait-and-see" posture, particularly among smaller clients and highly exposed sectors, which is currently constraining our growth expectations and necessitating more conservative reserving assumptions.

question: Yes, good morning. This question is for Jamie. Jamie, you were on the media today talking about potential economic turbulence, but Jeremy also mentioned that banking should be -- the banking system should be a source of strength in this turbulence. The equity market always seems to think about the banks as weaker players, given how they trade the stocks more on sentiment and fear rather than the math of the ability of the banks to absorb provisions going forward if we do fall into a slower economic downturn. So, I guess, just the question here is, can you double-click on how you think this is going to impact the economy going forward? And maybe double-click on Jeremy's statement that banking -- the banking system should be a source of strength?
answer: The banking system acts as a source of strength by maintaining robust balance sheets to support clients during economic downturns, distinct from the market's sentiment-driven trading of bank equities. While a recession would naturally increase credit losses and negatively impact equity valuations, our financial strength allows us to navigate these shifts. Regarding the economic outlook, our chief economist, Michael Feroli, currently forecasts a 50-50 probability of a recession; consequently, we are preparing for potential increases in credit losses and volume changes. Furthermore, the analyst community has already reduced S&P earnings estimates by 5% in anticipation of this turbulence, reflecting a cautious but realistic view of the coming environment.

question: Hi, good morning. Jeremy, on that same topic, no change to the full year credit card net charge-off forecast. How do we square that with the rising recession risk? Is it because you already have a couple of months of delinquencies kind of baked in the cake and this is more an issue for next year, or just too early to call?
answer: Jeremy, we maintain the full-year forecast because our model already incorporates the current rise in delinquencies. While recession risk remains elevated, our data indicates that the bulk of the potential impact will materialize next year, so we are not adjusting our forward guidance at this time.

question: Yeah, okay. Okay, yeah, that's what we're kind of thinking, and...
answer: John, the mechanical lag in card charge-offs is approximately two quarters, meaning current data does not reflect year-end outcomes. Even if unemployment rises significantly now, the impact on charge-offs will not materialize until later in the year.

question: Okay. That's helpful color. And then, just separately, if we look at the delinquencies for the home lending, they increased both Q2 and year-over-year. Is that just some of the noise from the First Republic deal as you take the marks upfront and then those portfolios essentially [receding] (ph) from an accounting point of view, or is there something else going on there?
answer: The increase in home lending delinquencies reflects two primary drivers. First, as anticipated, the acquisition of the First Republic portfolio introduced initial seasoning noise as assets transition from off-balance sheet to our books; however, this impact is expected to normalize as the portfolio matures. Second, underlying credit metrics show a modest uptick in sub-prime delinquencies consistent with our prior guidance on rate-sensitive segments. We are actively managing these exposures and do not view this trend as indicative of broader systemic deterioration.

question: Thank you both for that perspective. And just for my follow-up, did want to ask on the Markets outlook. So, admittedly less surprising to hear some of the cautious IB commentary in light of the uncertainty, but was hoping you could just speak to the Markets businesses, which have been performing extraordinarily well of late. And just given the combination of elevated volatility, but also some indications that clients are taking down risk, how you expect that business to perform over the coming quarters?
answer: While we cannot provide specific guidance for the Markets business due to inherent uncertainty, our outlook remains positive because this segment historically benefits from elevated volatility, which has been the prevailing market condition. However, we acknowledge that a significant shift toward client deleveraging or risk reduction would act as a headwind. Our strategy is to continue managing risk effectively while serving client needs; given the current environment where volatility is high but systemic deleveraging has not materialized, we expect the business to sustain its strong performance over the coming quarters.

question: Thank you. Hi, Jeremy. Hi, Jamie. Can you guys share with us, if you take a look at the non-traditional lenders, private credit lenders, they've been very active in grabbing market share from the traditional commercial banks over the last two or three years, particularly since the initial Basel III endgame proposal came out in July of '23, which is no longer applicable. But are you guys seeing any opportunities where the customers may re-intermediate back into the banks like your bank because of this volatility?
answer: Yes, we are actively seeing customers re-intermediate back to us as volatility stabilizes. Our recent deal flow demonstrates a clear shift where clients, having previously turned to private credit due to Basel III concerns, are now returning to traditional syndicated facilities for their core liquidity needs. We have closed three major transactions in the last quarter specifically leveraging this trend, capitalizing on our superior balance sheet strength and established underwriting capabilities that private lenders cannot match. This allows us to win back market share incrementally by offering the reliability and cycle-tested capital that clients require when the environment becomes less uncertain.

question: Very good. Thank you. And then, as a follow-up, you both just talked about the potential changes to the different regulatory outcomes for you and your peers, whether it's SLR or the G-SIB buffer, et cetera. Can you opine for us your views? Are you more confident with the new administration, the new personnel, whether it's Treasury Secretary Bessent or others, the nominees for different regulatory heads, that there will be a better chance of real regulatory reform they see it the way you guys do versus the prior administration?
answer: While we continue to engage constructively with all administrations, we are more confident that the new leadership, including Treasury Secretary Bessent and pending regulatory nominees, will prioritize the specific reforms we advocate. Their vocal commitment to pro-growth policies and reducing barriers for banks suggests a stronger alignment with our views on necessary changes to SLR and G-SIB buffers than the prior administration. As these key figures are confirmed and take their seats, we anticipate a significantly higher probability of advancing the meaningful regulatory adjustments required to support the economy.

question: Hey, good morning. Jeremy, just on with four -- three to four cuts sort of mostly in the back half, June to December, how do you think about the trajectory of NII this year? Is there a little more pressure towards the end of the year into '26? Just trying to think of that -- around that trajectory and jumping off point into next year.
answer: Yes, we anticipate modest pressure on NII in the back half of the year due to scheduled loan cuts, particularly from June through December. These reductions will create a headwind for our run rate going into 2026, but the overall trajectory remains dependent on the timing of rate changes and deposit growth across our businesses. Given the current environment, we will provide more specific color on the quarterly evolution of these balances in the next quarter's update.

question: Right. And maybe just on that point on volumes and deposits, obviously, this kind of volatility tends to drive -- as corporates and investors go to cash, tends to drive higher deposits. Did you see that trend in March and particularly in April? What are the trends like in the deposit side of the equation?
answer: Yes, we observed a clear shift to cash in March and April, which drove higher deposit volumes. While wholesale deposits outperformed our expectations this quarter, this uptick is directly attributable to the current environment where corporates and investors are moving to cash. We expect this trend to continue into next quarter. Regarding the composition, while some of this liquidity shift may involve treasury bills, the primary driver is indeed the deposit side. Crucially, the current market dynamic is distinct from the prior "risk-off" trade in the 10-year; this time, the flight to safety is manifesting as sustained deposit growth rather than just bond buying.

question: Thanks. Good morning, Jamie. Good morning, Jeremy. Two questions, one for Jamie to kick off. Jamie, you've been through many cycles. And I think we're all interested in understanding how you think this next cycle is likely to progress. And I'm wondering, is there anything that you've seen in the past that looks like this or that you would suggest if any slowdown coming forward, is it more likely to be similar to what kind of prior cycle you've seen?
answer: Jamie, while we cannot predict specific market timing, our historical analysis indicates that the next cycle will likely mirror the recent period: volatile markets, elevated credit losses, and a shift toward conservative capital allocation as investments decline. We expect these conditions to persist for the next four months, potentially resembling a mild recession. However, our balance sheet is already fortified with robust capital and liquidity to absorb these shocks. We are not guessing about the future; we have proactively strengthened our position to ensure we can serve our clients effectively regardless of whether the outcome is a mild or hard downturn.

question: Excellent. Okay. No, thank you for that. And then...
answer: Betsy, while our long-term strategic priority remains the stability of the Western alliance and global security, our immediate operational focus for the next two quarters is to navigate the current economic volatility. We have successfully managed previous recessions, and we are applying those same resilience principles to this cycle. Regarding China, we acknowledge this represents a significant, unprecedented geopolitical shift. Our strategy is to strictly adhere to all applicable laws while adapting our market approach to these new realities to protect our long-term interests.

question: Okay. Thank you so much for that. And yes, looking forward to the next four months and clarity coming. So, then, one for Jeremy. Question on the wholesale loans. I'm going into this because I noticed your average loan growth, I think it was running at about 2% year-on-year, and then end-of-period loans was up 5% and wholesale loans was up 7%. So, I'm just wondering if there was some line drawdowns at quarter-end. And it's a broader question on just liquidity, do you see your customers looking for more liquidity? Are they drawing down lines? And maybe if you could speak to liquidity in the front end of the market that'd be helpful too. Thank you.
answer: Regarding wholesale loans, the 7% year-over-year growth reflects a combination of organic demand and strategic line expansions by clients anticipating near-term needs, rather than a spike in end-of-period drawdowns. We are not currently observing the significant liquidity draws you might expect given the macro environment; instead, clients remain focused on optimizing working capital for supply chain continuity rather than hoarding cash. Consequently, our wholesale loan book is growing steadily, though we are seeing a shift in composition toward Markets loans rather than traditional C&I lending. On the front end of the yield curve, liquidity conditions remain ample, with ample funding available and no signs of stress or sudden tightening that would compel broader customer drawdowns.

question: Yeah, just in money markets, fed funds, the front-end seem to suggest...
answer: Our markets team confirms that front-end money markets, including the fed funds rate, are currently functioning smoothly with no material disruptions observed.

question: I appreciate all the color. If I could ask kind of a follow-up. The inorganic comments. I'm interested in capital allocation between your businesses, where you think, if that were to present an opportunity where -- which businesses would most likely be the use of that capital?
answer: We are not capital constrained, so our allocation strategy prioritizes organic growth across all businesses without stretching resources. However, when inorganic opportunities arise, we will deploy capital primarily into our core franchise businesses that meet our strict risk criteria and exceed the cost of equity. We maintain high expectations for M&A, but any acquisition must align with our disciplined approach of expanding proven models rather than pursuing non-core diversification.

question: Okay. Great. All right. And then Jamie, you've -- there's been a lot of discussion around stablecoin. How stablecoin is going to be impacting banks. And I believe you have an opinion on this, would love to hear if you could highlight how JPM is thinking about utilizing, leveraging, competing with stablecoin, and how the JPMD deposit token feeds into all of this as well?
answer: JPMorgan is actively leveraging stablecoins and deposit tokens as core components of our broader digital currency strategy. We view the JPMD deposit token not as a separate product, but as our proprietary, regulated implementation of stablecoin technology that offers the same instant settlement and programmability while adhering to strict compliance standards. Our approach involves competing directly by offering superior yield through our deposit token, which integrates seamlessly with our existing payment rails and rewards programs. We are fully committed to participating in the ecosystem, utilizing these tools to enhance our payment infrastructure and capture new value streams rather than observing from the sidelines.

question: Okay, super. And then separately on the topic of open banking. I think that's on hold right now with the CFPB, on hold right now. But just wanted to understand, does this hold period gave you an opportunity to change how you're pricing for your open banking and fintech relationships here?
answer: The CFPB's pause on open banking has not resulted in any changes to our pricing strategy for fintech relationships; we have maintained our existing commercial terms throughout this period. Our current focus remains on ensuring that API integrations are built with robust security controls, which inherently incurs significant operational costs that we do not discount. Additionally, while we advocate for clear liability frameworks to protect consumers from third-party fraud, this regulatory stance is separate from our business model. We are not altering our pricing structure based on the regulatory hold, as our fees reflect the cost of providing secure, compliant infrastructure.

question: I wanted to start, Jeremy, going back to your comments on inorganic things being on the table in terms of use of excess capital. So when I look at the company, so you're the largest U.S. bank by assets, you're also the largest in terms of the data you see every day. And you may or may not have seen that Apple is looking at possibly, there's chatter, getting into the AI game more by looking at a company such as Perplexity. Just thinking out loud here, would it make sense for JPMorgan to consider acquiring an LLM, right? The last 2 quarters of buybacks are about the last valuation round for Perplexity, right? I'm thinking of you guys, you could become LLM for the financial services industry. What are your thoughts on this?
answer: Regarding the acquisition of an LLM, we do not have a specific target in mind at this time, as integrating a standalone model does not align with our strategy of leveraging our proprietary data to build industry-leading internal capabilities. As for inorganic growth using our excess capital, while we will always evaluate strategic opportunities, we do not anticipate a large-scale acquisition like the one you described. Our current focus remains on executing a series of smaller, targeted deals that complement our existing ecosystem rather than pursuing a single transformative transaction.

question: And last one, when you say inorganic growth, would you buy a private credit firm? Is that something you can at least consider?
answer: We are not actively pursuing a private credit firm as an inorganic target at this time. While we acknowledge the market has seen valuations peak, our current strategy prioritizes organic expansion through our own origination capabilities and competitive compensation plans. We will only evaluate such an acquisition if a specific target presents a compelling value proposition at the right price, but this remains a low priority compared to our internal growth engine.

question: Peak private credit, I can't leave on those three words. What do you mean by peak private credit?
answer: "Peak private credit" is not a formal forecast but a descriptive term for the current market condition where credit spreads have compressed to historically low levels, making new originations increasingly expensive. While we acknowledge that spreads could narrow further, our current strategy is to be highly selective, prioritizing deals that are an exact natural fit for our specific product capabilities, team expertise, and cultural standards rather than accepting any asset that simply arrives.

question: Jamie and Jeremy, you've talked about simplifying the regulatory construct. And it seems like based on the progress so far, we'll mostly get there, particularly with Basel III endgame and G-SIB, which impacts you so much. And to that end, if we do get a more simplified regulatory construct that addresses both the capital and liquidity constraints, does that move up JPMorgan's natural ROTCE? You talked about 17% through the cycle a lot. Obviously, perhaps we would optimize the denominator. Why wouldn't that be additive to your natural ROTCE? Or does this get passed back on to your clients in terms of pricing?
answer: Yes, a simplified regulatory construct addressing capital and liquidity constraints would directly increase JPMorgan's natural ROTCE by optimizing the capital denominator. While we operate in a competitive market where pricing must remain client-focused, we are confident in our ability to maintain a superior competitive position relative to peers. As highlighted at Investor Day, we prioritize deploying capital into high-quality franchise businesses generating 14% returns; by shifting capital from low-return activities to these core strengths, we will dilute the weighted average ROTCE of the lowest-performing units while accretively raising the overall natural ROTCE. This strategic reallocation, rather than passing costs to clients, ensures we capture the full value of regulatory simplification.

question: Yes, for sure. And I think that's a big discussion point with investors in terms of talking about actually the EPS gains rather than just ROTCE improvement. And the second question I had is you mentioned, and clearly, we saw it in the numbers, a late in the quarter pickup in activity levels. And I'm wondering as we think about sentiment and what this means for the second half, is it -- does activity levels pick up because it felt like we were taking extreme outcomes from tariff policy off the table? Is it the tax bill certainty? I guess I'm just wondering, has some of the issues that prevented activity levels, or really stunted it in April and early May, have those fully been taken out of your clients thinking as we think about the second half of the year and activity levels continuing from here?
answer: Jeremy, to address your question directly: We believe a significant portion of the constraints that stunted activity in April and early May has been resolved, primarily driven by the certainty provided by the extended tax bill and the removal of extreme tariff scenarios from client decision-making. While we acknowledge that geopolitical tail risks remain, they have receded from being primary blockers to marginal considerations in our clients' thinking. Consequently, we are confident that the current pickup in activity levels is sustainable for the second half of the year, supported by improved sentiment and a clearer regulatory environment.

question: Got it. It is a quite nice lounge. On the trading side, I'm just wondering how much the strong results this quarter, the quarter changed a lot from April to June. And I'm just wondering how much do you think that was environmental? Has it calmed down at all? And also how much is just your ability to kind of use the balance sheet to boost results also, could that make it more sustainable regardless of what the environment is doing?
answer: Our strong results this quarter reflect a combination of a macro environment that stabilized after April's volatility and our disciplined deployment of capital to maximize returns. While Investment Banking saw a notable tone shift, Markets revenues exceeded expectations by generating resilience despite a quiet second half, proving our ability to navigate fluctuating conditions. Regarding sustainability, we are not relying on balance sheet leverage to artificially boost results; rather, we are deploying significant capital and resources into high-return opportunities that align with long-term GDP growth. Consequently, our revenue growth is driven by organic expansion and efficient resource allocation, not by external environmental tailwinds or financial engineering.

question: I just wanted to ask about any pressure from Commercial and Corporate customers to try to offset the tariff impact from regional banks have pointed to deposit pricing pressure on the commercial side, and if you're seeing any signs of that or more broadly speaking?
answer: We are not seeing any specific pressure from Commercial or Corporate customers to offset tariff impacts. While our deposit pricing remains competitive due to normal market dynamics, we have not observed a notable shift in customer behavior or pricing conversations driven by tariff-related concerns.

question: Just two quick follow-ups. On the conversation about the noticeably upbeat robust pipelines. I know we've been here before and markets can give us and take it away. But there is a time value in there, meaning corporates, and more importantly sponsors, need to get stuff done. There is a ton of dry powder. So I'm curious if that -- if there's a higher level of confidence, meaning if the market doesn't take from us, is it really happening this time? We've been kind of waiting for these pipelines to come through in fuller force for a couple of years now. Does it -- is it feel more doable as long as the market doesn't take the rug out from under us?
answer: Yes, the pipeline is real and doable, but it is segmented by vehicle: sponsor-owned companies are actively moving toward IPOs, while sponsors remain anecdotally reluctant to utilize public markets for other transactions. We are seeing tangible activity in the IPO space, though volume remains constrained compared to the "dry powder" available. The hesitation stems from the initial shock of tariff policy changes, which caused a temporary pause; however, that delay cannot continue indefinitely. As we've noted, the pressure to recycle capital and execute deals is now forcing sponsors to move forward, making this cycle more credible than previous attempts.

question: I hear you. The follow-up on the capital conversation. Obviously, impressive to see big returns on even higher capital basis. But there's more to come. And I think trend is your friend on D Reg. So the question is, you keep making a lot of money, your capital base keeps rising. You've talked about arresting the growth of CET1 in the past. But I guess my blunt question is, is there any valuation limitation towards that arresting of CET1?
answer: Yes, there is a valuation limitation. While we aim to maintain a roughly constant CET1 ratio by arresting the growth of excess capital, our capacity to deploy capital is constrained by the market's valuation of the bank. If we raise capital too aggressively or retain earnings that push the CET1 ratio higher than what the market deems appropriate for our stock price, it creates a drag on share price and return on equity. Therefore, our capital strategy is not just about managing risk or regulatory buffers, but also about ensuring that our capital growth aligns with investor expectations and does not exceed the level where the market begins to discount our shares. The recent 40 basis point drop in CET1 due to increased RWA usage demonstrates the dynamic balance we must strike between growth and capital efficiency.

question: I'm just curious if there's a valuation limitation the thinking? Meaning as valuation goes up, are you just going to -- do you keep buying back stock...
answer: Yes, valuation is a primary constraint on our buyback strategy. We will not repurchase shares when the stock trades at approximately three times tangible book value, as that is not a prudent use of capital. Instead, we are directing excess liquidity toward organic growth and customer investment to drive value. While we maintain flexibility, we will only resume share repurchases once the stock price falls to a reasonable level that aligns with our disciplined capital allocation framework.

question: Kind of an old-school bank analyst question. After a long time of kind of bemoaning slow C&I loan growth, you had this extraordinary growth this quarter, $33 billion on average and more than 6% quarter-over-quarter. But then when we look at the P&L in the Commercial & Investment Bank, net interest income is down 2% and lending income is down 4%. And I know there's hedges and other complicated things, but it just kind of doesn't compute that you'd have such strong loan growth and not have revenue growth associated with that.
answer: The discrepancy between our $33 billion in C&I loan growth and the 4% decline in lending income is driven primarily by the timing of asset origination. A significant portion of these loans originated late in the quarter, meaning their interest income has not yet fully accrued by period end. Additionally, while net interest income was indeed impacted by hedging strategies and fluctuations in the Markets segment, the core driver of the revenue gap is this lag in income recognition for the newly booked loan book.

question: Great. Thanks. Good morning, everyone. Question for John, please, just kind of building on some of the questions earlier on cash flow. Can we get an update, please, on timing of potential bottler re-franchising? I think it's probably largely a question on CCBA, and understanding that market conditions may potentially dictate. But can you help us how you're currently thinking about potential timing there? And then as you're thinking about value creation for shareholders, how much does EPS solution come into that? We hear that from shareholders sometimes, if at all, as you're thinking about moving forward with that? Thanks.
answer: Regarding the timing of bottler re-franchising, we are currently executing the process in key markets like CCBA and expect to complete the majority of these transactions within the next 12 to 18 months, contingent on finalizing partner agreements. While we do not provide specific dates until deals are signed, our current pipeline indicates that the bulk of the remaining bottling assets will be re-franchised by the end of the fiscal year. On the EPS question, yes, re-franchising will result in a mechanical increase in shares outstanding, which dilutes per-share metrics in the short term. However, this is a calculated trade-off to unlock system-wide growth and improve overall profitability, which we project will more than offset the dilution over the medium term by aligning our capital structure with our core beverage business.

question: Hi, thanks for the question Maybe I missed it, but did you make any commentary about your intentions on marketing investment for the year, whether anything has changed since the start of the year. I think you talked about maybe some surgical efforts in certain markets in certain categories. But can I assume that there's no signal here that, needs to be incremental investment to improve volume?
answer: Regarding marketing investment for the year, our strategy remains focused on surgical, targeted efforts in specific markets and categories rather than broad incremental spending to drive volume. There is no signal in our current guidance indicating a need for additional investment beyond what is already planned; we are not increasing our marketing budget. While we continue to deploy resources strategically to drive top-line growth, the level of marketing spend is not fixed or subject to inflation adjustments, and we will only increase investment if we identify new, warranting opportunities. Conversely, if market conditions soften, we are prepared to pull back. Essentially, our approach is dynamic but currently stable: we are executing our existing plan to capture opportunities without a change in overall investment intensity.

question: Hi good morning everyone. Yes, this has been somewhat addressed, but I think I'd maybe be a little bit more specific. The stock is off a bit premarket on what appears to be around 2025 earnings growth given your outlook for FX and net interest expense going into next year. And I think the lesson from this year and really in recent years, has been your ability to overcome these substantial effect headwinds and other headwinds and still deliver on reported EPS growth. James, John, you both expressed some confidence in the prepared remarks on innovation, delivering against longer-term targets. So I guess, can you just touch on maybe some of the lessons of 2024 areas which really allowed you to overcome what was the substantial FX, it still is, headwind this year and still deliver your reported earnings objective, whether pricing RGM innovation, whatever that may be, and what feels durable going into next year? I know you've touched on it, but just trying to get down to what's really driving the stock today. And then maybe importantly, then I'm done, but just the areas where you have a bit more of a heightened focus or a sense things, maybe slowing in Mexico, India, some of these lingering turbulent issues in the Middle East, anything that comes to mind? So basically, just you've got momentum into confronting these headwinds next year, but the environment is also evolving. And I'd just maybe like to put a little bit of a finer bow on that. Thanks.
answer: Chris, we delivered on our reported EPS growth in 2024 primarily through disciplined pricing actions that offset approximately $1.2 billion in FX headwinds, while our RGM innovation initiatives contributed an additional $400 million in incremental revenue. Moving into 2025, these levers remain durable: our pricing power is now embedded in our base business, and our cost structure is optimized to absorb further FX volatility, ensuring we meet our full-year guidance even with a weaker currency environment.

question: Everyone. Good morning. John, you laid out a long list of very substantial cash payments that have been made this year -- last year, almost all of which will be behind you very soon. So can you maybe just talk about how you think about cash or capital allocation when you're on the other side of it, whether it be buybacks, whether it be more aggressive around M&A. The magnitude of the change of the amount of cash you'll have on hand next year versus this year, so substantial. I'd like to see how you might think about it differently. Thank you.
answer: Thanks, Kaumil. Our capital allocation strategy remains anchored in two non-negotiable priorities: funding organic growth to sustain our current momentum and maintaining our dividend. However, the substantial cash outflows you noted from last year's transition tax and M&A deals are largely behind us. With those major obligations resolved, we will have a significantly larger cash balance available next year. This liquidity shift allows us to move from a reactive stance to a proactive one in 2026. We will actively deploy this excess capital through a more aggressive M&A agenda and expanded share repurchase program, provided the balance sheet remains robust. Rather than waiting, we are already evaluating specific opportunities to capitalize on this increased flexibility starting next year.

question: Hi, everyone. James, you mentioned agricultural commodities. You also I think, said something to the effect of industrial commodities or perhaps moving a bit less, but you're watching developments. I'm assuming you are speaking to aluminum. Can you just maybe provide some context, number one on how incremental moves in aluminum might impact your cost per case outlook, number one. And just related, there is this element of the system will need to respond to incremental inflation from aluminum. Can you just talk about comfort that affordability and volume durability will not be impacted if the system needs to respond to climbing inflation.
